In practical terms, the bill simply eliminates a sunset provision, ensuring the program cannot lapse due to inaction by Congress in the future.
ai_headline: VA homeless veterans mental health program made permanent
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: A bill to prohibit the Secretary of Labor from finalizing, implementing, or enforcing a proposed standard with respect to heat injury and illness prevention, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would permanently block the U.S. Department of Labor from completing, putting into effect, or enforcing a workplace safety rule that the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) proposed in August 2024, which was designed to protect workers from heat-related injuries and illnesses in both outdoor and indoor settings. The bill also prevents OSHA from issuing any other rule that is substantially similar to that proposal. The proposed rule that would be blocked had included requirements such as scheduled rest breaks in high heat, procedures to help workers gradually adjust to working in the heat (called acclimatization), and written heat safety plans for employers. The bill would affect any employer that OSHA oversees — across all industries and work environments — by ensuring that no new federal heat safety standards based on the 2024 proposal can be created or enforced. Workers in jobs with significant heat exposure, such as construction, agriculture, manufacturing, and warehousing, would also be directly affected, as the federal protections that rule was intended to provide would not go into effect.
ai_headline: Senate bill bars OSHA heat safety rule from taking effect
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

title: To nullify certain rules related to foreign assistance.
ai_summary: This bill would cancel three specific federal rules issued by the Department of State in January 2026 that place certain restrictions and requirements on U.S. foreign aid programs. The three rules being canceled deal with: (1) policies described as "protecting life" in foreign assistance (which relate to abortion-related restrictions on aid recipients), (2) policies targeting what the rules call "discriminatory equity ideology," and (3) policies targeting what the rules call "gender ideology" in foreign assistance. If passed, these rules would be treated as if they never existed, and no federal agency would be allowed to enforce them or create similar replacement rules. The bill primarily affects U.S. government agencies that distribute foreign aid — such as the State Department — as well as the foreign organizations and governments that receive that aid and would have been subject to the canceled rules.
ai_headline: Bill would repeal three State Dept. foreign aid rules
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

title: Reducing Arbitrary Barriers to Apprenticeship Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill changes how veterans' education benefits are paid out when a veteran chooses an apprenticeship or on-the-job training program instead of a traditional college degree. Under current law, veterans in these programs receive a reduced housing stipend compared to those attending college full-time; this bill would fix that by paying apprentices the full housing allowance based on where their employer is located, putting them on equal financial footing with college-going veterans. The bill also removes a minimum attendance requirement — meaning veterans in construction-trade apprenticeships (such as carpentry, electrical work, or plumbing) would still receive their monthly benefits even in months when they fall below a certain number of training hours, a flexibility not previously available. These changes apply to veterans using Post-9/11 GI Bill benefits, veterans using the Montgomery GI Bill (active duty), and members of the Selected Reserve using their education benefits.
ai_headline: GI Bill apprenticeship benefits would match college rates
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: To require the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to establish an education program on unleaded aviation gasoline, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill directs the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) to create and run a public education program about unleaded aviation gasoline — the fuel used in small, non-commercial aircraft — within one year of the law taking effect. The program must publish easy-to-find information online covering which unleaded fuels are approved for use, how compatible those fuels are with different aircraft and engines, safety guidance, and any available tax credits or other government incentives. The FAA must also work with the aviation industry and fuel suppliers to offer annual hands-on training for the people who handle and dispense aviation fuel at small airports. The program affects a wide range of people in general aviation, including pilots, flight instructors, aircraft owners, mechanics, airport managers, and fuel service providers. The program would run until the end of 2036, and the FAA must periodically report to Congress on how the program is going, including how much unleaded fuel is being sold compared to the traditional leaded fuel known as 100LL.
ai_headline: FAA ordered to educate pilots on unleaded aviation fuel
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

title: To strengthen the public-private partnerships and policy efforts of the Department of State to advance women's economic security in South and Central Asia, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill directs the U.S. Secretary of State to maintain and grow partnerships between the U.S. government, businesses, universities, and civil society groups aimed at improving women's economic opportunities in South and Central Asian countries. It creates a new unit inside the State Department's Bureau of South and Central Asian Affairs, led by a Special Advisor for Women's Economic Security with the rank of Ambassador, who would oversee these partnerships and report to senior State Department leadership. The partnerships are meant to help women in the region gain better access to jobs, entrepreneurship resources, education, financial services, mentorship, and ownership of assets. U.S. diplomatic posts in relevant countries would also be required to designate a staff member to track private-sector commitments made through these partnerships. Within one year of the bill becoming law, the State Department would need to report to Congress on what steps were taken, what partnerships exist, and what economic impact the work has had.
ai_headline: State Dept. would gain women's economic envoy for Asia
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

title: To reauthorize the active transportation infrastructure investment program, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would continue federal funding for a program that builds sidewalks, bike lanes, trails, and other infrastructure meant for people who walk, bike, or use other non-motorized ways of getting around. Specifically, it would authorize $250 million per year from the Highway Trust Fund for each year from 2027 through 2031 — a total of up to $1.25 billion over five years — to keep the program running after its current funding period ends. The money cannot be moved to other programs and does not expire until it is spent. The people most directly affected are communities and local governments that apply for these grants to build or improve walking and biking infrastructure, as well as residents who use those facilities.
ai_headline: Bill would fund walking and biking trails through 2031
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

title: To prohibit the enforcement of certain contractual clauses that restrict disclosure of sexual abuse of minors, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill makes it illegal to enforce any contract clause that prevents a victim of child sexual abuse — or someone supporting that victim — from speaking out about the abuse. In plain terms, if someone was sexually abused as a minor, any "keep quiet" or confidentiality agreement they signed (or that was signed on their behalf) cannot be used to stop them from telling law enforcement, child protection agencies, courts, or anyone else about what happened. The bill applies to contracts already in existence, not just new ones, meaning old settlement or nondisclosure agreements cannot be used to silence survivors going forward. Importantly, the bill still allows contracts to keep certain details confidential — such as the dollar amount of a legal settlement — as long as those restrictions do not prevent a survivor from disclosing the abuse itself. This affects sexual abuse survivors who are minors or were minors at the time of abuse, people with knowledge of such abuse, and anyone — such as an institution or alleged abuser — who might otherwise try to enforce a secrecy clause against them.
ai_headline: NDAs shielding child sex abuse lose legal enforceability
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: A bill to establish requirements for financial aid offers made by institutions of higher education, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill sets rules for how colleges and universities must present financial aid offers to students. Schools that receive federal funding would be required to send financial aid offers in a clear, standardized format that lists costs first (broken into what the school directly charges and other estimated expenses), then shows grants and scholarships the student does not have to repay, then calculates a "net price" showing what's left to pay, and finally — in a clearly separate section — shows any recommended student loans with plain disclosures that loans must be repaid. The bill directs the Department of Education to develop standard definitions and terminology for financial aid terms, create a model offer form through consumer testing with students and families, and prohibit schools from calling a financial aid offer an "award" or mixing loans together with grants in a way that could confuse students about what is free money versus debt. Schools would annually submit their financial aid offer templates to the Department of Education, which would make them publicly available, and the Government Accountability Office would later study whether the forms are working well and recommend improvements. The bill affects all students and prospective students at federally funded colleges and universities, as well as the institutions themselves.
ai_headline: Senate bill mandates clearer college financial aid forms
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

title: A bill to direct the Federal Communications Commission to establish a program to make grants available to States to inform individuals of potential eligibility for the Lifeline program of the Commission, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill directs the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) to create two grant programs aimed at getting more low-income people enrolled in the Lifeline program, which is a federal program that helps qualifying low-income households pay for phone and internet service. In the first program, the FCC would award competitive grants to states, territories, tribal governments, and the District of Columbia to fund outreach efforts — such as informing eligible people about Lifeline, helping them apply, and partnering with community organizations — so that people who qualify but haven't signed up yet actually learn about and can access the benefit. In the second program, the FCC would provide grants to any state or tribal government that applies within 30 days of the law's enactment, specifically to connect their existing government benefit databases (like food stamp records) to the National Lifeline Eligibility Verifier, making it easier to automatically confirm whether someone qualifies for Lifeline. The people most directly affected are low-income individuals and households who may be eligible for discounted phone or internet service but are not currently enrolled, as well as the state and tribal agencies that administer benefit programs.
ai_headline: FCC grants would expand Lifeline enrollment outreach
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation. (text: CR S2131-2132)

title: To amend the Congressional Budget and Impoundment Control Act of 1974 to require the Congressional Budget Office to include the effects of using baseline assumptions when preparing estimates under such Act, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill requires the Congressional Budget Office (CBO) — the independent agency that calculates how much proposed laws will cost the federal government — to add extra information to its cost estimates explaining how its built-in assumptions affect the numbers it reports. Specifically, whenever the CBO estimates the cost of a bill, it must also show how much the estimate would differ if those built-in assumptions (called "baseline assumptions") were not used, identify which specific assumptions caused any difference, and flag any cases where those assumptions make a program look cheaper or free when the government may actually be expected to spend real money on it. This matters because the CBO's baseline sometimes assumes that certain government programs, benefits, or payment rates will continue as-is, which can make a bill appear to cost less than it otherwise might. The bill primarily affects how the CBO presents its work to Congress, and indirectly affects any lawmaker, journalist, or citizen who relies on CBO cost estimates to understand the financial impact of proposed legislation.
ai_headline: CBO must disclose how baseline assumptions shape cost estimates
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Rules, and in addition to the Committee on the Budget, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: A bill to modernize clinical trials and remove barriers for participation in clinical trials, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill makes it easier for people — especially those from groups that have historically been left out of medical research, such as rural communities, tribal areas, and people of lower income — to participate in clinical trials for drugs, devices, and vaccines. It allows drug and device companies to legally pay trial participants' out-of-pocket medical costs (like copays and deductibles) as well as travel, transportation, and meal expenses without those payments being treated as illegal kickbacks or fraud under federal law, as long as specific safeguards are followed. It also allows companies to provide free digital health tools (such as monitoring devices or apps) to participants who need them to take part in a trial. Additionally, money that participants receive from a clinical trial sponsor is excluded from their taxable income, up to $2,000 per year. Finally, the bill authorizes federal grants to fund community outreach, education, and training — including in multiple languages — to recruit more diverse participants and clinical trial investigators.
ai_headline: Bill would ease barriers to joining clinical trials
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

title: Carlton H. Ingram Veterans’ Benefits Protection Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) measures the severity of a veteran's service-connected disability when rating it for compensation purposes. Specifically, it requires the VA to evaluate how disabling a condition actually is *without* the masking effect of medication or treatment — meaning if a drug or therapy is controlling or reducing a veteran's symptoms, the VA cannot simply rate the disability based on those improved, treated symptoms alone. The idea is that if evidence exists to show how bad the condition would be without treatment, the VA must use that baseline to determine the disability rating. The bill also makes clear that veterans can still seek additional compensation if their medication or treatment itself causes new disabilities or makes a pre-existing service-connected condition worse. This affects veterans who have service-connected disabilities and are receiving treatment that might otherwise make their condition appear less severe than it truly is, potentially protecting them from receiving lower disability ratings — and therefore lower benefits — simply because their treatment is working.
ai_headline: VA must rate disabilities without factoring in treatment
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: To amend the Federal Power Act and the Public Utility Regulatory Policies Act of 1978 to require investor owned electric utilities and gas utilities and transmission providers to, when establishing or calculating a return on equity, establish or calculate the return on equity at the lowest return on equity in an established range of reasonableness, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill changes how much profit investor-owned electric and gas utilities — that is, privately owned power and gas companies — are allowed to earn on the money their shareholders have invested, which directly affects the rates customers pay on their utility bills. Instead of allowing regulators to set that profit rate anywhere within an acceptable range (as current practice allows), the bill would require it to be set at the *lowest* point in that range, calculated using a five-year average of expected U.S. stock market returns as estimated by academic programs, large financial institutions, and major global banks. If a utility can prove with strong evidence that a higher profit rate is truly necessary to attract investment and stay financially healthy, an exception can be made, but the utility must publicly explain and quantify the difference in dollar terms, including the impact on the average household's monthly bill. The bill also bars utilities from passing on to customers the costs of lobbying, political contributions, advertising meant to shape public opinion, executive travel, entertainment, rate-case legal fees, investor relations, and fines or penalties. Electric cooperatives and publicly owned utilities (such as those run by cities or states) are not covered by this bill — it applies only to privately owned, investor-owned utilities.
ai_headline: Bill limits utility profit rates and customer fee pass-throughs
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

title: A bill to amend title 38, United States Code, to require the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to develop a five-year human capital plan to support the mission and responsibilities of the Department of Veterans Affairs and to require the Secretary provide notice before carrying out a reduction in force, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill requires the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) to create a detailed, five-year staffing plan — updated every year — that maps out how many workers the VA needs, in what roles and locations, to deliver health care, benefits, and other services to veterans. Before the VA can lay off or reduce its workforce, it must give 60 days' advance written notice to Congress and to the affected employees, explaining which offices and jobs are being cut, why, how the cuts align with the staffing plan, and what the budgetary effects will be; employees who don't receive proper notice cannot legally be let go until they do. The bill also requires the VA to report to Congress after any office reorganization, tracking whether the changes actually improved services using measurable results. The Government Accountability Office (an independent watchdog) must review the staffing plan at least every two years and report its findings to Congress. The people most directly affected are VA employees — particularly those facing potential layoffs — and veterans who rely on VA health care, benefits, and other services.
ai_headline: VA staffing cuts would require 60-day notice to Congress
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: Presumptive CLARITY Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would require the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) to post on a public website a running list of medical conditions and groups of veterans it is actively considering when deciding whether to add or remove "presumptive" service connections related to toxic exposure — meaning situations where a veteran's illness is automatically assumed to be linked to their military service without them having to prove it individually. For each condition or veteran group on the list, the VA would also have to explain where that item stands in the review process and how the public can submit comments or recommendations. The VA would have 180 days after the bill becomes law to launch this public webpage. This bill primarily affects veterans who have been exposed to toxic substances during their service, as well as their families and advocates who want to track and weigh in on VA decisions that could determine whether certain illnesses qualify for benefits.
ai_headline: VA must publish toxic exposure review status online
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: To authorize the Secretary of State to provide funds for a United States pavilion or other major exhibit at any international exposition or world's fair, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill allows the U.S. Secretary of State to use federal government money to build and operate a U.S. pavilion at international world's fairs and expositions, removing a longstanding rule from 1994 that blocked federal funding for that purpose. Before spending any of that money, the State Department must notify key congressional committees at least 15 days in advance and provide details including where the funds are coming from, how much private money is also expected, and what economic investment the pavilion might attract to the U.S. Any companies hired to build or run the pavilion must certify they are following the host country's labor laws, anti-corruption laws, and are not using victims of human trafficking. Within 180 days after a pavilion opens, the Secretary of State must report back to Congress on how many U.S. businesses participated and how much outside funding was raised. This bill primarily affects the State Department, U.S. businesses that might showcase their products at world's fairs, and American taxpayers who would now be funding these international exhibits.
ai_headline: Federal funds restored for U.S. world's fair pavilions
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

title: To require the Secretary of Health and Human Services to award grants to support community-based coverage entities to carry out a comprehensive coverage program that provides qualifying individuals and small businesses health coverage and integrated health related social need services to small business workers that promote improved health, long-term economic self-sufficiency, employment and retention, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill directs the federal Department of Health and Human Services to award between 3 and 5 grants to nonprofit community organizations to run local health coverage programs for people who earn too much to qualify for Medicaid but cannot afford typical private insurance — generally workers at small businesses whose household income is up to 4 times the federal poverty level. These programs would provide medical coverage (including doctor visits, hospital care, mental health services, prescriptions, and preventive care) with no deductibles and limited co-pays, with the cost shared among government, local hospitals, employers, and the enrolled workers themselves. Beyond just health insurance, the programs would also assign participants a personal health coach and connect them to community resources — such as job training, education, and social services — to help them improve their health and become more financially self-sufficient over time. The grants would last 4 years, and funding authorized in the bill ranges from $4.8 million in the first year up to $12 million per year in years three and four.
ai_headline: HHS grants would fund local coverage for uninsured workers
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: A bill to require the Under Secretary of Commerce for Oceans and Atmosphere to identify a consistent, Federal set of best available forward-looking meteorological information and to require the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology to convene an effort to make such set available, with advice and technical assistance, to standards-developing organizations, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill directs two federal agencies to work together to help buildings and infrastructure be better prepared for extreme weather and other environmental risks. First, the head of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) would be required to gather and maintain an up-to-date, government-wide set of weather forecasting data — covering future risks like hurricanes, extreme heat, flooding, wildfires, and rising sea levels. Then, the head of the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) would be required to make that data available to the public and to share it, along with expert guidance, with the private organizations that write the rules used to design buildings and set safety standards — so that those rules can account for what weather conditions are expected to look like in the future, not just what they have looked like in the past. A working group of representatives from relevant federal agencies would also be created to support the research needed to keep this weather information current and useful. The people most directly affected would be professionals in construction, engineering, and building safety, though the long-term goal is to make homes, buildings, and communities more resilient for everyone who lives and works in them.
ai_headline: NOAA and NIST to align climate data with building codes
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

title: To require certain duties and covered import restrictions with respect to forestry products to be reduced by 50 percent if an importer of such product can demonstrate that such product contains 100 percent raw wood material originating in the United States, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would cut certain import taxes and fees on finished wood products — things like lumber, plywood, flooring, furniture parts, and wooden tools — by 50 percent, but only if the company importing those products can prove to U.S. Customs that every bit of the raw wood used to make them originally came from the United States. It also requires the President to give at least 90 days' public notice in the Federal Register before imposing or changing any such import taxes on wood products, and must allow at least 60 days for businesses and the public to submit written comments before those changes take effect. The bill directs U.S. Customs and Border Protection, working with the U.S. Trade Representative, to write rules within 90 days to verify importers' claims and prevent fraud. The people most directly affected are companies that export American timber or raw wood to other countries for manufacturing and then import the finished products back into the United States, as well as any businesses or individuals who would otherwise pay the full import tax on those finished wood goods.
ai_headline: Bill would halve tariffs on U.S.-origin wood reimports
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

title: Veterans Health Administration Novel Therapeutics Preparedness Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the Department of Veterans Affairs to create a new office — the Office of Novel Therapeutics — inside the Veterans Health Administration to help the VA get ready for new mental health treatments, particularly therapies that are currently being reviewed by the FDA (such as certain psychedelic-assisted therapies). The office would set national policies, safety standards, training requirements, and care models so the VA is prepared to offer these treatments to veterans if and when they receive federal approval. The bill also calls for a Clinical Implementation Program to test how well these treatments actually work in real VA settings, and allows the VA to designate certain medical centers as "centers of excellence" to lead research and training on these therapies. A new advisory committee made up of veterans, their family members, and health experts would provide input on patient safety and access. This primarily affects veterans dealing with conditions like PTSD, depression, substance use disorders, and traumatic brain injury, as well as the VA clinicians and staff who would be trained and certified to deliver these treatments.
ai_headline: VA bill targets new mental health therapies for veterans
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: To provide for a procedure for Congress to carry out a reversal of a decision by the President not to declare a major disaster requested by the Governor of a State under the Robert T. Stafford Disaster Relief and Emergency Assistance Act, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: When a state governor asks the President to declare a major disaster and the President says no — especially when that refusal goes against the recommendation of FEMA or against established practice — this bill would give Congress a way to override that decision. Within 24 hours of such a refusal, the President would be required to send a written explanation to congressional leaders explaining why. Congress could then pass a special type of joint resolution, with strict time limits and fast-track rules in both the House and Senate, to force the disaster declaration to go through anyway. This affects state governments seeking federal disaster aid, residents of states that have been denied disaster declarations, and the balance of authority between the President and Congress over how federal disaster relief is granted.
ai_headline: Bill would let Congress override presidential disaster denials
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure, and in addition to the Committee on Rules, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: To encourage the development of specialized foster care programs designed specifically for large sibling groups, sibling groups with a wide age range, and sibling groups with complex needs.
ai_summary: This bill directs the federal Department of Health and Human Services to create a pilot grant program that gives money to organizations working to keep large sibling groups together in foster care. Up to five grants can be awarded over a five-year period, with a total of no more than $10 million available, and the money must be used to build specialized foster care programs focused on three specific situations: sibling groups of three or more children, siblings with a wide age difference, and siblings where one or more children has complex needs (such as a serious mental health condition, a disability, a life-threatening illness, or a history of trauma). Organizations that can apply include state child welfare agencies, tribal and local agencies, faith- and community-based organizations, and nonprofits — as long as they have proven experience running foster care programs and operate under state oversight. Grant recipients must report back to the federal government on how many children were served, how siblings were placed together, and what the outcomes were, and they may only spend the funds on evidence-based approaches that have been shown to meaningfully increase the number of siblings placed together.
ai_headline: HHS pilot grants would fund keeping siblings in foster care together
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

title: Produce Prescriptions for Veterans Act
ai_summary: This bill would allow the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) to provide "produce prescriptions" as part of its medical services for eligible veterans. A produce prescription would work by giving a veteran — who both has a diet-related chronic condition (such as diabetes or heart disease) and struggles to afford food — a voucher or debit card to buy fruits and vegetables. The goal is to help improve the veteran's health by making it easier for them to eat a healthier diet. Veterans who are food-insecure and have a qualifying diet-related condition would be the ones directly affected by this change.
ai_headline: VA bill targets food insecurity with produce vouchers
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: Veterans’ Bill of Rights Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would require the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) to actively inform veterans about their rights when receiving VA health care, benefits, and services. Specifically, the VA would have to make sure veterans know they have rights such as being treated with respect, receiving clear information about their treatment and benefits, applying for benefits at any time, filing complaints without fear of retaliation, and appealing decisions they disagree with. To carry out this, the VA would need to train all its employees on these rights every year, display them prominently at VA facilities and on its website, add them to the mobile app and benefits portal within 180 days of the law taking effect, include a lesson about them in the military-to-civilian transition program, and attach a summary of these rights to any claim or health care application acknowledgment. This bill affects all veterans who interact with the VA, as well as VA employees who would receive new training requirements — though the bill explicitly states it does not create new legal rights to sue the VA or change who is eligible for VA benefits.
ai_headline: VA bill would publicize veterans' rights in care and benefits
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: To prohibit the imposition of any duty on the importation of phosphate fertilizers under section 122 or 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would remove certain import taxes (tariffs and countervailing duties) on phosphate fertilizers brought into the United States from Morocco, starting within days of the bill becoming law. Specifically, it cancels two types of existing charges: special trade-related tariffs that had been applied under presidential trade authority, and a countervailing duty order from 2021 that was originally put in place because Morocco was found to be subsidizing its fertilizer exports in ways that hurt U.S. producers. It also requires the U.S. government to refund import fees that companies already paid under that 2021 order, as long as those fees weren't finalized through a completed government review. The people most directly affected are U.S. businesses that import Moroccan phosphate fertilizers and American farmers who buy phosphate fertilizers as an input for growing crops.
ai_headline: Bill would scrap tariffs on Moroccan phosphate fertilizers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

title: ZOMBIE Act
ai_summary: This bill changes the rules that federal agencies must follow when tracking and reporting money that the government pays out incorrectly, with a new focus specifically on payments that actually cause the government to lose money — mainly through fraud — rather than payments that were simply processed with a paperwork error but went to the right person for the right amount. Under the new rules, agencies would need to assess the fraud risk of every federal program before sending out money, publish those risk assessments at least once every three years, and include estimates of financially harmful improper payments in their annual budget documents. A senior official at each major agency would be required to meet regularly with budget, treasury, and inspector general officials to report on what steps are being taken to prevent fraud and recover lost funds. The bill primarily affects federal executive agencies and their employees who manage payment programs, but it also indirectly affects taxpayers and anyone who receives federal benefits or payments, since the goal is to ensure government money reaches only the right people for the right reasons.
ai_headline: Federal agencies face new fraud-focused payment rules
last_action_text: Committee Consideration and Mark-up Session Held

title: Veterans Appeals Improvement and Modernization Act 2.0
ai_summary: This bill makes several changes to the process veterans use when they disagree with a VA decision about their benefits and want to appeal it. It gives veterans more flexibility to switch between appeal tracks or withdraw an appeal without losing their place in line, and it extends the window for submitting evidence to 90 days after a hearing or after filing a notice of disagreement. It also requires the VA to send appeal decisions electronically by default and to write those decisions more clearly, spelling out what evidence was considered, what findings favored the veteran, and exactly what the veteran would need to show to win their claim. The bill directs the VA to review and improve its electronic case-management systems, requires an outside organization to study the appeals process and recommend ways to reduce cases being sent back for corrections, and asks the Government Accountability Office to examine how well the VA puts court rulings into practice. Finally, the VA would have to publish an annual public report breaking down the outcomes of appeals so veterans and the public can see how different types of cases are decided.
ai_headline: Senate bill would ease VA benefits appeals process
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: To require the Commander of the United States Space Command to submit a feasibility report on expanding the Multinational Force Operation Olympic Defender.
ai_summary: This bill requires the top commander of U.S. Space Command to study and report to Congress — within one year — on whether it is practical and beneficial to bring Japan, South Korea, and potentially other U.S. allies in the Asia-Pacific region into an existing multinational military space cooperation program called Operation Olympic Defender. The report must cover what activities are already underway toward that goal, what policy changes those countries would need to make, what funding or rule changes the U.S. would need, and how adding these countries would affect U.S. national security. The bill does not actually expand the program or commit any funding — it only orders the study and the report. The people most directly affected are military and government officials who would conduct the study, though the findings could eventually influence decisions about how the U.S. cooperates with Asian allies on space defense.
ai_headline: Space Command ordered to study Asian allies' role in Olympic Defender
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

title: To provide a gasoline tax holiday.
ai_summary: This bill would temporarily eliminate the federal gasoline tax for consumers from the date it becomes law through the end of 2026. Normally, a federal excise tax is charged on gasoline when it is produced or sold, and this bill would set that tax rate to zero during that period, meaning fuel sellers would not owe that tax to the federal government on gasoline sold in that window. To make sure roads and environmental cleanup programs don't lose funding, the bill requires the U.S. Treasury to transfer money from the government's general fund directly into the Highway Trust Fund and the Leaking Underground Storage Tank Trust Fund to replace what would have been collected. The bill also states that it is Congress's intent for gas stations and fuel producers to pass the savings along to customers at the pump, and it gives the Treasury Department authority to enforce that expectation.
ai_headline: Federal gas tax suspended through end of 2026
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

title: To amend title 31, United States Code, to authorize pausing and segmenting payments, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill gives federal agencies and the Treasury Department the legal authority to temporarily pause, add conditions to, or split up government payments when there is an objective, documented reason to suspect fraud or an improper payment. When a payment is flagged — either by the agency itself, by a state or local government administering a federally funded program, or by the Treasury's automated fraud-detection system — the agency must notify the person or organization expecting the payment within two days, explain why the payment was paused, and give them a chance to contest the decision. If the agency confirms the payment is legitimate, it must release the funds within 30 days (or within 7 days of a successful challenge by the payee). The bill protects government employees who act in good faith under these rules from personal legal liability, and it requires the Treasury Department to report annually to Congress on how many payments were paused, how many were ultimately released, and how much money was saved by catching fraudulent payments. This affects anyone who receives payments from the federal government — including individuals, businesses, and state and local governments that distribute federal funds — as well as the federal employees who process and certify those payments.
ai_headline: Federal agencies gain power to pause suspected fraud payments
last_action_text: Committee Consideration and Mark-up Session Held

title: Dakota Mainstem Water Supply Project Feasibility Study Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the U.S. Secretary of the Interior to work with a nonprofit organization called the Dakota Mainstem Regional Water System to study whether it would be practical and affordable to build a new water supply project serving communities across parts of South Dakota, Iowa, Nebraska, and Minnesota. The study would look at providing water for cities and towns, rural areas, and businesses in that region. The federal government would pay no more than half the cost of the study, with up to $10 million in federal funding authorized for that purpose. The authority to carry out this study would expire ten years after the bill becomes law.
ai_headline: Federal study eyed for Dakotas regional water supply
last_action_text: Subcommittee Hearings Held

title: To direct the Secretary of the Interior to convey to the Nisqually Indian Tribe the Clear Creek Hatchery infrastructure.
ai_summary: This bill would transfer ownership of the Clear Creek Hatchery — a fish hatchery facility in Washington State — from the federal government to the Nisqually Indian Tribe, free of charge. Within 90 days of becoming law, the U.S. Department of the Interior would be required to hand over all federal rights and ownership of specific hatchery structures, including fish ponds, dams, a fish ladder, springs, wells, water infrastructure, raceways, roads, and fencing at the site. The federal government would prepare and make publicly available an official map and legal description of exactly what is being transferred, and if there is any conflict between those two documents, the map takes priority. This transfer primarily affects the Nisqually Indian Tribe, who would become the new owners of the hatchery infrastructure, and the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, which currently manages the property.
ai_headline: Federal hatchery transfers to Nisqually Indian Tribe
last_action_text: Subcommittee Hearings Held

title: To establish governmentwide requirements for pre-payment fraud prevention actions, to provide the U.S. Treasury appropriate data resources, to facilitate participation in governmentwide anti-fraud data sharing, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill requires federal agencies to run a series of fraud checks on every payment before it is approved and sent out — verifying, for example, that the payee is real and not deceased, that bank account information is correct, and that money is available in the right account. To support those checks, it gives the U.S. Treasury Department new access to data from the IRS, the Social Security Administration, and the National Directory of New Hires, and it strengthens an existing government database called the "Do Not Pay" system that flags potentially fraudulent or ineligible payment recipients. It also requires any organization or individual receiving a federal award of $50,000 or more for the first time to file a one-time report explaining how they used the money, and agencies must stop further payments to recipients who fail to file. These changes affect all federal agencies that send out payments or grants, as well as state and local governments administering federally funded programs, private businesses and nonprofits that receive federal awards, and anyone whose information is held in the relevant government databases.
ai_headline: Federal agencies required to verify payments before sending funds
last_action_text: Committee Consideration and Mark-up Session Held

title: A bill to authorize the East Wing Modernization Project, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill sets aside $400 million in federal funds to modernize the East Wing of the White House, covering design, construction, and related costs for improvements such as a secure ballroom and a visitor screening facility. The money is available through January 20, 2029, and is meant for national security-related upgrades to that part of the White House complex. To help pay for this spending, the bill extends two existing fees that international travelers and importers already pay at U.S. borders — customs user fees and merchandise processing fees — by three months, pushing their expiration from the end of December 2031 to the end of March 2032. This primarily affects the federal government and White House operations, while the fee extension continues costs that businesses and travelers importing goods into the United States currently pay.
ai_headline: Senate bill funds East Wing White House renovations
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

title: A joint resolution proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States to protect United States citizenship.
ai_summary: This proposal would change the U.S. Constitution to narrow who automatically receives American citizenship at birth. Right now, the 14th Amendment grants citizenship to nearly everyone born on U.S. soil; this amendment would add a new requirement — at least one parent must be a U.S. citizen or national, a lawful permanent resident ("green card" holder) living in the U.S., or a non-citizen serving in the U.S. military on active duty. Children born in the United States to parents who don't meet any of those three conditions would no longer automatically receive birthright citizenship. This would most directly affect children born to parents who are in the country temporarily (such as on a visa) or without legal immigration status. Because this is a constitutional amendment, it would need approval from two-thirds of both the Senate and the House, and then ratification by three-fourths of all state legislatures, before it could take effect.
ai_headline: Senate bill would limit 14th Amendment birthright citizenship
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

title: A bill to establish the National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill directs the National Science Foundation to create a shared national platform called the National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource (NAIRR) that gives U.S. researchers, educators, and students access to computing power, data, software, AI models, and training materials they need to conduct AI research and build AI skills. The platform would be open to people affiliated with universities, nonprofits, government agencies, federally funded research centers, and certain small businesses, but would be off-limits to anyone working on behalf of specific foreign countries identified as national security concerns. Access would generally be free at a basic level, though the office running the platform could charge fees to some users to help cover costs, and any fees collected could be reinvested back into the program. An advisory committee would be created to provide oversight and recommendations on how the platform is managed, and the National Science Foundation would be required to publish a public annual progress report.
ai_headline: NSF would build shared AI research platform for U.S. academics
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

title: A bill to nullify certain rules related to foreign assistance.
ai_summary: This bill would cancel three specific rules that the Department of State published in January 2026 related to how the U.S. distributes foreign assistance funding. The three rules being canceled dealt with restrictions tied to abortion and reproductive health services (sometimes called a "Mexico City Policy" or "global gag rule"), requirements to avoid what the rules called "discriminatory equity ideology," and restrictions related to "gender ideology" in foreign aid programs. If this bill became law, those three rules would be treated as if they had never existed, and no federal agency would be allowed to enforce them or create replacement rules that are substantially similar. The people most directly affected would be foreign organizations and individuals who receive U.S. foreign aid — particularly those involved in healthcare, public health, and human rights work — as well as the U.S. government agencies that administer foreign assistance programs.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets three State Dept. foreign aid rules
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to the withdrawal of the rule relating to "Debt Collection Practices (Regulation F); Pay-to-Pay Fees".
ai_summary: Congress is voting to block a decision made by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) in May 2025, in which the CFPB canceled a rule about "pay-to-pay fees" — the extra charges that debt collectors sometimes impose on people who want to pay their debts by phone or online rather than by mail. The original rule, which the CFPB had issued in 2022, would have placed limits on those convenience fees; the CFPB later withdrew that rule in 2025, effectively allowing those fees to continue without federal restrictions. This resolution says that the CFPB's decision to withdraw the rule should have no legal effect, which would restore the 2022 rule and its protections. The people most directly affected are consumers who owe debts and are charged extra fees when they choose certain payment methods, as well as the debt collection companies that charge those fees.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets CFPB rollback of debt fee limits
last_action_text: Star Print ordered on the reported joint resolution.

title: Veterans Affairs Peer Review Neutrality Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill requires that when the Veterans Health Administration (VHA) reviews the quality of care a doctor or other health care provider gave to a veteran, the people doing that review must be neutral and free from conflicts of interest. Specifically, anyone involved in the peer review process must step aside if they personally participated in the care being reviewed or if they cannot be objective for any reason. The bill also addresses a particular situation where the provider being reviewed is themselves a member of the peer review committee at their facility — in that case, the initial review must be sent to a separate, neutral peer review committee at a different VHA facility, so colleagues are not essentially judging each other's work. These rules apply to VHA medical facilities and the staff and committee members who conduct peer reviews across the system. The goal is to make sure quality reviews of veteran care are handled fairly and without bias.
ai_headline: VHA peer reviews would require conflict-of-interest rules
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: A bill to prohibit gender transition procedures on minors, to authorize the Secretary of Health and Human Services to impose civil penalties on persons who perform gender transition procedures on minors, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would make it illegal for any doctor or medical professional to perform gender transition procedures — such as puberty-blocking drugs, hormone treatments, or surgeries — on anyone under 18 years old, anywhere in the United States. If a provider violates this ban, the federal government's health department can fine them at least $100,000 per violation, and the minor (or their parent or guardian) can also sue the provider in federal court for medical costs, emotional distress, and potentially additional punitive damages — with up to 25 years after the minor turns 18 to file a lawsuit. The bill exempts certain medical care from this ban, including treatment for intersex conditions, emergency procedures that prevent death or serious bodily harm, and puberty suppression used specifically for the recognized medical condition of precocious (abnormally early) puberty. The bill also creates a grant program, funded by the collected fines, that gives money to private nonprofit organizations to help individuals who want to reverse a prior gender transition procedure, providing them with medical advice, mental health services, and education or employment assistance — though any organization that performs or refers for gender transition procedures (or most abortions) is ineligible for those grants.
ai_headline: Senate bill would ban youth gender transition care
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

title: A bill to amend the Higher Education Act of 1965 to require the use of an identity fraud detection system in reviewing Free Applications for Federal Student Aid.
ai_summary: This bill requires the U.S. Department of Education to use an automated identity fraud detection system to review every FAFSA (Free Application for Federal Student Aid) submitted starting October 1, 2026, looking for signs that someone may be using a fake or stolen identity to apply for financial aid. If an application raises a red flag, the Department must notify both the applicant and any college the applicant listed, explaining that the application is under suspicion. Before that college can hand over any federal financial aid money, it must first confirm the applicant's identity in person or through a live video call, and then report that verification back to the Department. The bill affects college-bound students applying for federal aid, the Department of Education, and colleges and universities that receive and distribute federal financial aid. The Department is also required to report to Congress on how the system works and how effective it is, both before it launches and on an annual basis afterward.
ai_headline: Education Dept. to screen FAFSA applications for identity fraud
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

title: A bill to amend the Clayton Act to provide for the divestiture of certain transactions, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill requires that large corporate mergers and acquisitions worth $10 billion or more that took place between January 20, 2025, and January 19, 2029, be undone — meaning the merged companies must be separated back into independent businesses — unless the companies can prove in court that the deal did not significantly reduce competition in any market. It also allows federal antitrust agencies and state attorneys general to review any merger from that same time period, even smaller ones, and order those to be unwound if there is evidence the government's review process was improperly influenced — for example, by political pressure, conflicts of interest, foreign interference, or misrepresentations to regulators. Companies that fail to complete a court-ordered separation on time can face daily fines, penalties tied to the deal's total value, and fines against individual executives and board members, and if no one complies a court-appointed trustee can take over and sell off the assets. The bill also extends the time limit for bringing antitrust lawsuits from four years to ten years, giving the government and private parties a longer window to challenge problematic deals.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets $10B mergers made under Trump
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

title: Dennis and Lois Krisfalusy Act
ai_summary: This bill expands who can receive a free government-issued memorial headstone or marker from the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA). Currently, certain family members of veterans — such as spouses or dependents who were buried somewhere other than a national cemetery — can only get one of these markers if they died on or after November 11, 1998. This bill removes that date restriction, meaning eligible family members who died **before** that date would now also qualify for a VA-furnished memorial marker. In practice, this could allow veterans' families to finally obtain an official government marker for loved ones who passed away years or decades ago and were previously excluded simply because of when they died.
ai_headline: VA memorial markers extended to families of veterans who died before 1998
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: Women Veterans Specialty Care Access Act
ai_summary: Women veterans who are enrolled in VA healthcare would be able to book their own appointments for certain women's health services — specifically gynecology, obstetrics, maternity, and postpartum care — without first needing a referral or approval from a primary care doctor. This applies both to care provided directly by VA facilities and to care received through the VA's community care program, which allows veterans to see outside providers. Veterans could schedule these appointments by phone, online, or through any other booking method the VA already offers. The VA would also be prohibited from adding any other extra steps or approvals that could slow down access to these services.
ai_headline: VA women veterans gain direct access to gynecologic care
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: Veteran Burial Benefit Correction Act
ai_summary: This bill raises the amount the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) pays toward burial and funeral costs when a veteran dies from a disability directly caused by their military service. Currently, the VA pays up to $2,000 in those cases; this bill would increase that limit to $3,000. Going forward, the $3,000 cap would also automatically adjust each year to keep pace with inflation, using the same Consumer Price Index measure that tracks general price changes across the country. This change affects the families or estates of veterans whose deaths are officially linked to a service-connected condition, as they would receive a larger reimbursement to help cover funeral and burial expenses.
ai_headline: VA burial benefit for service-connected deaths rises to $3,000
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: To clarify that the baseline is based on current laws and the assumption of continuation of current levels of discretionary appropriations, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill changes the rules for how the federal government calculates its official budget "baseline" — the forecast of future spending and revenues used as a starting point for budget decisions. Under the new rules, the baseline must assume that current spending levels simply continue as-is, without automatically adding projected increases for inflation or other factors that the current method includes. It also removes certain other adjustments, such as assuming that some temporary or emergency spending will keep growing over time. The Congressional Budget Office (CBO), which produces these projections, would also be required to publish alternative budget scenarios alongside its main forecast, developed in consultation with the House and Senate Budget Committees. This primarily affects how Congress, budget analysts, and policymakers measure whether new legislation increases or decreases spending relative to the baseline, which in turn can influence how proposed cuts or increases are scored and debated.
ai_headline: Bill would reshape how CBO calculates spending baselines
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Budget.

title: To amend the Fair Credit Reporting Act to prohibit consumer reporting agencies that furnish consumer reports for tenant screening purposes from providing certain information, to establish duties of users of consumer reports for housing purposes, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill changes what information can be included in a background check report when a landlord is deciding whether to rent housing to someone. Specifically, it would prohibit tenant screening companies from reporting a long list of items, including arrests that didn't lead to a conviction, juvenile records, successfully completed diversion programs, any conviction for which a person has already finished their sentence or is on probation or parole, fines or fees related to court costs or incarceration, and any record that has been expunged, sealed, or pardoned. The bill also requires landlords who reject a rental applicant based on a background check to notify that applicant within three days and explain specifically what information in the report led to the denial. The people most directly affected are renters and rental applicants — particularly those with any kind of criminal history — as well as landlords and the companies that provide tenant screening reports.
ai_headline: Bill would limit criminal history in tenant screening reports
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

title: To require States to hold special elections to fill vacancies in the House of Representatives not later than 180 days after a vacancy occurs in the House of Representatives, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: If a seat in the U.S. House of Representatives becomes empty — because a member dies, resigns, or is unable to serve — this bill would require the affected state to hold a special election to fill that seat within 180 days of the vacancy opening up. The only exception is if a regularly scheduled general election for that same seat is already happening within that 180-day window, in which case the state can simply wait for that election. The bill affects state governments, which currently have wide flexibility in deciding when (or even whether) to hold such special elections. To make sure states follow the new deadline, the bill allows the U.S. Attorney General, private citizens harmed by a violation, or even the House Speaker or Minority Leader to sue the governor of a non-complying state in federal court to force compliance.
ai_headline: House vacancy elections must occur within 180 days
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.

title: To clarify the eligibility of certain student populations for child care assistance under the Child Care and Development Block Grant Act of 1990.
ai_summary: This bill expands who can qualify for federally funded child care assistance by clarifying that parents who are enrolled in certain educational or job-training programs — including programs accredited by a college or university, programs at schools accredited by a Department of Education-recognized agency, apprenticeships or training programs registered with the Department of Labor or a state labor agency, and programs approved by a state to train and certify nursing assistants — are considered to be engaged in an approved "activity" that makes them eligible for child care help. Before this change, the eligibility rules were less specific about which types of programs counted, which may have left some student-parents uncertain about whether they qualified. The bill primarily affects low-income parents who are pursuing education or vocational training and need financial help paying for child care while they attend their programs. It also authorizes $850 million per year in additional funding for child care assistance from 2027 through 2031 to help cover the costs of serving these families.
ai_headline: Student-parents gain clearer path to federal child care aid
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

title: To amend the National Nutrition Monitoring and Related Research Act of 1990 to codify the Dietary Guidelines for Americans, 2025-2030, issued jointly by the Secretary of Agriculture and the Secretary of Health and Human Services, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would change how the federal government's official dietary guidelines — the recommendations that tell Americans what to eat for good health — are updated and put into effect. Right now, the Secretaries of Agriculture and Health and Human Services can issue updated guidelines on their own every five years; under this bill, they would still develop and propose new guidelines, but those guidelines could only take effect if Congress formally approves them by passing a law. The bill also locks in the current **Dietary Guidelines for Americans, 2025–2030** (issued January 7, 2026) as the official standard, and states that those guidelines cannot be changed or replaced unless the new process is followed. This affects anyone whose life is touched by federal nutrition policy, including the roughly 40 million Americans who participate in federal food programs like school lunches, SNAP, and WIC, since those programs are required to align with the Dietary Guidelines.
ai_headline: Bill would require Congress to approve dietary guidelines
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

title: To prohibit the use of funds to implement, administer, or enforce measures requiring certain employees to refer to an individual by the preferred pronouns of such individual or a name other than the legal name of such individual, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would bar the use of federal money to carry out, manage, or enforce any rule, policy, or guidance that requires federal employees or contractors to use a person's preferred pronouns (when those pronouns differ from the person's biological sex as defined in the bill) or to call someone by a name other than their legal name. In practical terms, this means federal agencies could not make it a workplace requirement for their staff or contractors to use pronouns or names that differ from a person's legal identity as defined by these standards. Any federal employee or contractor who believes their agency has violated this restriction can file a written complaint, and if the agency's response is unsatisfactory, they can sue the agency in court within one year of the alleged violation. A court could order the agency to stop the practice, award the employee money for damages, and require the government to pay the employee's attorney fees, with any punitive damages capped at $100,000.
ai_headline: Bill would ban federal pronoun mandates for workers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

title: Joint Medical Facilities Fund Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill creates a permanent, shared bank account — called the Joint Medical Facility Fund — that the Department of Defense (DoD) and the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) can both pay into and draw from to run medical facilities they operate together, serving both active-duty military and veterans under one roof. Both departments can deposit money into the fund from their regular budgets, and the fund can also receive money collected from insurance companies or other third parties when care is provided at these shared facilities. The money in the fund can be used for day-to-day operations, equipment, building upkeep, and smaller construction projects at these joint facilities, with most funds available for about one year and a small portion (up to 2%) lasting an extra year. The bill also requires the two departments to agree on a shared financial management process so each side can track its own contributions, and within 180 days of becoming law, both departments must report to Congress on which additional facilities could potentially become joint facilities in the future.
ai_headline: DoD and VA would share a joint medical facility fund
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: A bill to amend the Animal Welfare Act to establish additional requirements for dealers, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would require licensed dog dealers — primarily large-scale commercial breeders — to meet stricter care standards for the dogs they keep and breed. On the housing side, dealers would need to provide solid floors, enough space based on each dog's size, enclosures that aren't stacked on top of each other, and temperatures kept between 45 and 85 degrees Fahrenheit. Each dog would also be entitled to daily outdoor exercise, at least 30 minutes of human and social interaction per day, twice-daily feedings, constant access to clean water, and regular veterinary care including annual exams and vaccinations. The bill places new limits on breeding practices, such as capping how many litters a female dog can have in her lifetime, setting minimum and maximum ages for breeding based on the dog's size, and requiring health screenings before any breeding attempt. Finally, dealers would be required to make genuine efforts to find adoptive homes for dogs retired from breeding, rather than selling them at auction or transferring them to other breeders.
ai_headline: Senate bill sets new welfare rules for licensed dog dealers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry. (text: CR S2131)

title: A bill to amend the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 to allow for limited full expensing of certain reforestation expenditures.
ai_summary: This bill changes federal tax rules to make it easier and more financially beneficial for landowners to replant trees on their property. First, it raises the cap on reforestation costs that can be immediately deducted on taxes — from $10,000 to $30,000 for most filers, and from $5,000 to $15,000 for married couples filing separately — and ties those limits to inflation going forward so they keep pace with rising costs. Second, it creates a new, separate tax deduction specifically for landowners who need to replant trees that were damaged or destroyed by a presidentially declared natural disaster, such as a wildfire, hurricane, or flood, allowing deductions of up to $500,000 per property and up to $1,000,000 total across all properties, also adjusted for inflation over time. This disaster-related deduction comes with a catch: if a landowner sells the replanted property within 10 years, a portion of the tax benefit must be paid back, though exceptions exist for casualties, government takings, or the death of the owner. These changes would primarily affect private forest landowners, timber businesses, family farms with woodland, and similar property owners who invest in tree planting.
ai_headline: Senate bill raises reforestation tax deduction limits
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

title: Veterans Outdoor Rehabilitation Act
ai_summary: This bill would require the Department of Veterans Affairs to create a grant program that sends money to each state's main veterans agency so those agencies can build or expand outdoor recreation programs for veterans. Each qualifying state agency that applies would receive at least $200,000 per year, and the bill sets aside up to $10 million per year total for the program. States could use the money in various ways — such as hiring local guides or outdoor outfitters, covering veterans' equipment and transportation costs, running outreach to let veterans know about the programs, and partnering with federal land agencies like the National Park Service or the Forest Service to find suitable locations. The outdoor activities must be structured and nature-based, aimed at improving veterans' mental and physical health, and must be accessible to veterans with disabilities. In return for the funding, state agencies must track and annually report data such as how many veterans participated, what activities were offered, and whether participants reported improvements in well-being.
ai_headline: VA outdoor recreation grants target veteran wellness
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: A bill to amend the Higher Education Act of 1965 to provide for comprehensive student achievement information.
ai_summary: This bill would require colleges and universities to publicly report — and share directly with their students — more detailed information about how long it actually takes students to finish their degrees or certificates. Currently, schools mostly report graduation rates based on whether students finish in 150% of the normal program length (for example, within 6 years for a 4-year degree); this bill would expand that to also show rates at the normal expected time, at 200%, and — for shorter programs — at 300% of the normal time. The reporting would also be broken down by four types of students: first-time full-time students, first-time part-time students, returning full-time students, and returning part-time students, so prospective students can see outcomes for people in situations similar to their own. The bill also requires that all these percentages be displayed in a consistent way and with equal visibility, so no single figure gets buried or highlighted over others. This primarily affects colleges and universities that receive federal financial aid funding, as well as the students and families who use published school data to make decisions about where to enroll.
ai_headline: Colleges would report fuller graduation timelines under S 4431
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions. (text: CR S2130-2131)

title: Thomas M. Conway Veterans Access to Resources in the Workplace Act
ai_summary: This bill requires employers with 50 or more employees to post a notice in their workplace — in the same area where other required workplace notices are typically displayed — that lists benefits available to military veterans. The federal Departments of Labor and Veterans Affairs would work together to create a separate version of this notice for each state, including information about federal veterans' benefits, how to apply for them, the Veterans Crisis Line, and any additional benefits offered by that particular state. The notice, sized to fit a standard sheet of paper, must be reviewed and updated at least twice a year, and states are given opportunities to contribute and update their own information. The posting requirement applies to private businesses, government agencies, and certain federal entities like the Government Accountability Office and Library of Congress, as long as they employ at least 50 people.
ai_headline: Employers would post veterans' benefits notices at work
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

title: A bill to provide for a procedure for Congress to carry out a reversal of a decision by the President not to declare a major disaster requested by the Governor of a State under the Robert T. Stafford Disaster Relief and Emergency Assistance Act, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill creates a way for Congress to override a presidential refusal to declare a major disaster in a state, but only under specific circumstances. When a state governor requests a federal disaster declaration and the President says no — either by going against FEMA's recommendation to approve it, or by following a FEMA recommendation to deny it that itself breaks with past practice — the President must send a written explanation to Congress within 24 hours. After receiving that explanation, members of Congress have 14 days to introduce a joint resolution that would force the President to issue the disaster declaration, and the bill sets out a fast-track process for both the House and Senate to debate and vote on that resolution quickly, with limited debate time and no amendments allowed. This bill primarily affects states that have been denied federal disaster aid, the President, FEMA, and members of Congress who would gain this new oversight tool.
ai_headline: Senate bill lets Congress override disaster denial
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

title: To direct the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to issue regulations to include strollers in the contract of carriage of air carriers and set a liability limit for damaged strollers, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would require the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) to create new rules making strollers an officially recognized item in the legal agreements (called "contracts of carriage") that airlines have with their passengers, ensuring strollers are treated as a standard part of the travel experience rather than an afterthought. Airlines would also be required to tag strollers with a label indicating they should be handled with care. If an airline damages a stroller, the bill sets a specific compensation limit of $2,175 that a passenger could claim, and this amount would count within the overall baggage liability limit airlines are already required to follow. These rules would apply to all air carriers and would need to be consistent with international aviation rules that govern flights between countries.
