The people most directly affected are individuals with injury, environmental, or product-harm claims against large companies, as well as corporations that use complex restructuring tactics (sometimes called "divisional mergers" or "Texas Two-Steps") to try to limit their legal liability through bankruptcy court.
ai_headline: Senate bill tightens rules on corporate bankruptcy abuse
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

title: Dietary Supplement Listing Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would require companies that make, package, or distribute dietary supplements sold in the United States to register each of their products with the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) by submitting key information — including the product's name, ingredients, serving sizes, directions for use, allergen warnings, and a copy of the label. The FDA would then create a publicly searchable online database where consumers could look up this information for any listed supplement, though certain details like contact information for company personnel and the exact amounts of individual ingredients within proprietary blends would be kept confidential. Companies already selling supplements when the law takes effect would have 18 months to complete their listings, while newly launched products would need to be listed before they go on sale. Any supplement sold without a proper listing on file would be considered mislabeled under federal law, meaning the company could face legal consequences. This bill primarily affects dietary supplement manufacturers, distributors, and retailers, while also giving everyday consumers easier access to information about the supplements they buy.
ai_headline: FDA would list all dietary supplements in public database
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

title: Answering the Call Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill directs the federal government to take specific steps to encourage first responders — including police officers, firefighters, emergency medical technicians, and 911 dispatchers — to use the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline when they need mental health support. The Department of Health and Human Services would be required to run outreach campaigns, award grants to fund awareness efforts, train 988 hotline counselors to better understand the unique experiences of first responders, and collect anonymous data to see how well the hotline is actually being used by this group. The bill also establishes a pilot program in which the federal mental health agency and the U.S. Fire Administration would work together with first responder organizations to make sure these outreach efforts are well-suited to the people they're trying to reach. Privacy protections are built in, meaning any data collected cannot identify individual callers and must follow existing federal and state privacy laws. Within three years of the law taking effect, the government would be required to report to Congress on how the pilot program worked.
ai_headline: Federal push to connect first responders with 988 crisis line
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

title: RECOVER Act of 2026
ai_summary: Under current Medicare rules, when a patient receives multiple therapy services (such as physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech therapy) in the same session, Medicare pays the full rate for the first service but reduces payment by 50 percent for additional services provided at the same time. This bill would eliminate that 50 percent payment cut starting January 1, 2027, so that Medicare would pay the full rate for all therapy services provided in a single session, regardless of how many are given at once. The change applies to services billed under both the standard Medicare physician fee schedule and the outpatient therapy and rehabilitation program. The people most directly affected are Medicare patients who need multiple types of therapy at once — such as stroke survivors or people recovering from serious injuries — as well as the therapists and outpatient rehabilitation facilities that treat them, who currently receive reduced reimbursement for those additional services.
ai_headline: Medicare would end therapy payment cuts for combined sessions
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: PEARL Act
ai_summary: This bill would allow any licensed pharmacist — whether working for a private pharmacy or a government agency — to refuse to dispense or sell the drugs mifepristone and misoprostol when those drugs are being used for abortion services, if doing so conflicts with the pharmacist's sincerely held religious beliefs. Under this protection, a pharmacist who refuses on those religious grounds could not be penalized in any way, including losing their pharmacy license or having federal funding withheld from their employer. If a pharmacist is nonetheless punished or forced to fill such a prescription despite this protection, the bill gives them the right to sue in federal court to stop that punishment or requirement. The bill affects licensed pharmacists across the country, as well as the employers, licensing boards, and government agencies that currently have authority over pharmacists' professional conduct.
ai_headline: Pharmacists could refuse abortion drug sales on religious grounds
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues

title: Economy of the Future Commission Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill creates a temporary, bipartisan advisory group — made up of 12 members appointed by Congressional leaders plus four nonvoting Deputy Secretaries from the Departments of Education, Labor, Commerce, and Treasury — to study how artificial intelligence is changing the U.S. economy and recommend specific laws Congress could pass in response. The group is required to produce two public reports: a shorter one within 7 months estimating how AI might affect jobs and federal tax revenue over the next 5 to 10 years, and a fuller final report within 13 months with concrete legislative recommendations on topics such as worker retraining, unemployment insurance, education, taxation, manufacturing, transportation safety, and energy demand. The four cabinet departments receiving the final report must each respond with their own written assessment within 60 days. The group shuts down 120 days after it delivers its final report, and the entire effort is funded with $5.25 million set aside for this purpose. This bill most directly affects workers whose jobs could be disrupted by AI, businesses — especially small and medium-sized ones — that use or compete with AI, and policymakers who would act on the group's recommendations.
ai_headline: Bipartisan panel would study AI's impact on U.S. jobs
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, and in addition to the Committees on Energy and Commerce, Transportation and Infrastructure, Oversight and Government Reform, and Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Prison Libraries Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would create a federal grant program, run by the Attorney General, that gives money to states and U.S. territories to build or improve libraries inside prisons. The grants could pay for things like books, computers, internet access, librarians, educational classes, job training, and cultural programs — all available to incarcerated people at no cost to them. States that apply must show they have a real plan, demonstrate a need based on data about their prison population, and report back each year on how the money was spent. The program is capped at $10 million per year for six years, and no single grant can last longer than six years total.
ai_headline: Senate bill funds prison libraries across U.S. states
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

title: To prohibit liability against those engaged in the mining, extraction, production, refinement, transportation, distribution, marketing, manufacture, or sale of energy for damages or injunctive or other relief from the use of their products, and for other purposes.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: Breast Cancer Stamp Reauthorization Act
ai_summary: This bill extends the life of a special U.S. postage stamp that raises money for breast cancer research. The stamp — called a "semipostal" stamp — costs a little more than a standard stamp, and that extra amount goes toward breast cancer research funding rather than just paying for postage. Under current law, the Postal Service's authority to sell this stamp was set to expire in 2027; this bill would push that deadline to 2037, allowing the stamp to remain on sale for an additional ten years. Anyone who buys postage stamps is potentially affected, since they would continue to have the option to purchase this stamp and contribute to breast cancer research through their everyday mail.
ai_headline: Senate bill extends breast cancer stamp sales to 2037
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

title: Stop Climate Shakedowns Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would make it illegal for anyone — including state governments, cities, or private citizens — to sue oil, gas, coal, and other fossil fuel companies in either federal or state court for harms connected to climate change, such as flooding, wildfires, or extreme heat. Any such lawsuits already in progress when the bill becomes law would have to be immediately thrown out, and any existing state or local laws designed to make energy companies pay for climate-related costs would be cancelled and made unenforceable. The bill declares that only the federal government — not states, cities, or individuals — has the authority to regulate greenhouse gas emissions and climate change. This would affect energy companies (which would be shielded from these types of legal claims), as well as states, cities, and people who might otherwise try to use the courts to seek compensation or other relief for climate-related damages.
ai_headline: Senate bill bars climate suits against fossil fuel firms
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

title: Clean Slate through Consolidation Act
ai_summary: If a federal student loan borrower is in default and then takes out a Federal Direct Consolidation Loan to pay off that defaulted loan, this bill would require the government or whoever holds the loan to ask credit bureaus to remove the default record from the borrower's credit history. In other words, consolidating the defaulted loan would effectively give the borrower a "clean slate" on their credit report for that default, as if it never happened. This affects federal student loan borrowers who have defaulted on loans originally made, insured, or guaranteed under federal student loan programs and who choose to consolidate those loans. Currently, even after a borrower resolves a default through consolidation, the record of that default can remain on their credit report and hurt their credit score — this bill would eliminate that outcome.
ai_headline: Federal loan consolidation would erase default from credit reports
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

title: FAITH Act
ai_summary: This bill would make it a federal crime to charge someone money — or withhold goods, services, or opportunities from someone — because of their religious beliefs, membership in a religious group, or refusal to join one. For example, it would be illegal for any person or organization to impose a fee, fine, or financial penalty on someone simply because they do or don't belong to a particular religion. The punishment ranges from a fine and up to one year in prison for smaller amounts (up to $1,000), up to three years in prison for larger amounts. The bill makes clear that religious organizations and schools can still ask members for voluntary donations for their own internal purposes — that remains fully legal. It also adds this new crime to the list of offenses that can be prosecuted under federal organized crime (RICO) laws, meaning the crime could be pursued as part of a larger criminal enterprise if applicable.
ai_headline: Bill targets financial penalties based on religion
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues

title: Drug Origin Transparency Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill requires drug companies to provide more detailed information about where their drugs and drug ingredients come from — and to make that information visible to the public on drug labels. Specifically, manufacturers would have to report to the federal government not just that they made a drug, but also who supplied each ingredient and how much of the drug was made using ingredients from each supplier; these reports could be required up to four times a year instead of just once. In addition, drug labels and accompanying documents would have to show the name, location, and a unique identifying number for the original manufacturer of the drug or its active ingredients, as well as any other companies involved in manufacturing, packing, or distributing it. The people most directly affected are drug manufacturers, ingredient suppliers, and distributors, who would face new reporting and labeling obligations; consumers and pharmacists would benefit from greater visibility into where drugs and their components are actually made. The Food and Drug Administration would be responsible for writing the specific rules that spell out exactly what information must be reported and how it must appear on labels.
ai_headline: FDA would require drug supply chain labels to go public
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

title: Securing America’s Drug Supply from Communist China Act
ai_summary: This bill would require the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to screen drug applications — both new ones and those submitted since January 1, 2016 — to determine whether the company seeking approval has financial or organizational ties to the Chinese government, Communist Party, or military. If a company is found to have such ties, the FDA would be blocked from approving its drug applications, and U.S. Customs and Border Protection would be required to turn away and destroy any of its drugs already approved and being shipped into the country. Companies flagged under this law would have a chance to prove they've cut those ties or sell their approved drug licenses to an unaffiliated company within 180 days to avoid import bans. There is a limited exception: if blocking a drug would cause or worsen a drug shortage in the United States, Customs could allow it in anyway. The bill primarily affects pharmaceutical companies with connections to China, as well as U.S. agencies responsible for drug approvals and border enforcement, and could indirectly affect American patients and pharmacies that rely on drugs made or owned by Chinese-linked companies.
ai_headline: FDA would ban drugs tied to Chinese government
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

title: Hemp Safety Enforcement Act
ai_summary: This bill gives states and Native American tribes the option to step outside the federal hemp regulatory system and run their own rules for growing and selling hemp and hemp-derived products (such as CBD gummies or other cannabinoid goods). Instead of submitting a detailed plan to the U.S. Department of Agriculture and following federal hemp rules, a state or tribe can simply send a notice saying it wants to handle regulation on its own — as long as it requires a minimum age for buying hemp-derived products. Once a state or tribe takes that path, it keeps its own definitions of "hemp" and related products, meaning it can set stricter or different standards than the federal government does. The bill also protects the flow of hemp products across state lines: no state or tribe can block the shipment of hemp goods to or from a self-regulating state or tribe, though products moving between two self-regulating places must follow the rules of both. This affects hemp farmers, hemp product businesses, and consumers in any state or tribal territory that chooses to opt out of the federal program.
ai_headline: States could manage their own hemp rules under new bill
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

title: FLEX Act
ai_summary: This bill changes the federal rules for how money is distributed and used to support charter schools across the country. It adjusts how federal funding is divided among different charter school programs — raising the minimum shares set aside for certain purposes — and allows that money to be used for a broader range of activities, including adding new academic programs or expanding offerings at already high-performing charter schools, not just opening new ones. The bill also makes it easier for charter schools to receive grant money upfront (rather than waiting to be reimbursed), reduces paperwork requirements for schools and the organizations that oversee them, and limits the federal government to issuing only regulations that are strictly necessary. It also requires that states consult with charter school operators before new rules are proposed, and adds a requirement that charter schools receiving federal funds address how their students will get to and from school. The people most directly affected are charter school operators, the state agencies that manage charter school grant programs, and the students who attend or may in the future attend charter schools.
ai_headline: Senate bill rewrites federal charter school grant rules
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

title: Securing Local Communities Input in Broadband Development Act
ai_summary: This bill would cancel two specific FCC decisions from 2018 that were designed to speed up the rollout of 5G and other broadband infrastructure by limiting what state and local governments could require from telecommunications companies seeking to install equipment. By wiping out those FCC rulings, the bill would restore the ability of state and local governments — such as city councils and county boards — to set their own rules and conditions when companies want to place cell towers, antennas, or other broadband equipment on public property or rights-of-way. The people most directly affected would be local governments, residents whose communities host that infrastructure, and telecommunications companies that currently operate under the looser federal standards the 2018 rulings established. In short, the bill shifts decision-making power over broadband infrastructure placement back toward local communities and away from the federal government.
ai_headline: Bill would restore local control over 5G tower siting
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

title: Ending the Carried Interest Loophole Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how certain investment fund managers — such as those who run private equity, hedge, or venture capital funds — are taxed on a special type of pay called "carried interest," which is the share of a fund's profits they receive as compensation for managing the fund. Under current law, that profit share is often taxed at the lower long-term capital gains rate; this bill would instead require those managers to pay ordinary income tax rates on the portion of their profits that exceeds what a normal financial return on their own invested money would produce. To calculate how much of a manager's earnings count as compensation (rather than as a return on investment), the bill uses a formula based on a benchmark interest rate plus nine percentage points applied to the fund's total invested capital. The bill also closes related workarounds, such as using loans from the partnership or restructuring the interest through financial contracts, so that managers cannot avoid the new rules through alternative arrangements. This primarily affects people who earn carried interest from managing investment partnerships — not ordinary investors, employees, or small business owners.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets carried interest tax break for fund managers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

title: Pedophile Financial Accountability Act
ai_summary: This bill would require the head of a federal financial watchdog agency — the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN), part of the U.S. Treasury Department — to investigate whether banks and their employees broke federal anti-money laundering laws in connection with financial transactions involving Jeffrey Epstein. The investigation would specifically look at whether banks, including JPMorgan Chase and Bank of America, were too slow to file required reports on suspicious transactions, failed to question large payments involving Epstein and certain wealthy individuals named in the bill, and whether senior bank executives allowed employees to keep doing business with Epstein even after the banks had dropped him as a client due to money laundering concerns. Within 100 days of the bill becoming law, FinCEN would have to deliver a report to Congress on what it found, and that report could include information drawn from confidential suspicious activity reports filed about Epstein-related transactions. If the investigation uncovers evidence that any bank employee may have willfully broken federal anti-money laundering laws, FinCEN would be required to refer those individuals to the U.S. Attorney General for possible further legal action.
ai_headline: FinCEN ordered to probe Epstein bank transactions
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

title: HERO Child Care for Military Families Act
ai_summary: This bill takes several steps to make child care more available and reliable for military families. It allows national service volunteers (such as AmeriCorps participants) trained in education to work at military child development centers, lets two part-time workers split a single full-time child care job (working at least 20 hours each per week), and creates a system where people can complete their background checks and health screenings *before* a job opening exists, so hiring can happen faster when spots do open up. Child care workers at military centers could also gain access to certain on-base benefits — such as commissary and exchange privileges, fitness centers, tuition assistance, and referral bonuses — if the Defense Department decides those benefits are needed to attract and keep qualified staff. The bill also requires the Defense Department to build a nationwide data system tracking child care capacity, staffing vacancies, waitlists, and unmet need across all military installations, and to report to Congress on the connection between child care availability and military readiness, troop retention, and military spouse employment. The people most directly affected are military families seeking child care, current and prospective child care workers at military installations, and military spouses.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets military child care staffing gaps
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.

title: YouthBuild for the Future Act
ai_summary: This bill continues federal funding for the YouthBuild program, which helps young people from disadvantaged backgrounds earn a high school diploma or GED, learn job skills, and work on building or repairing affordable housing in their communities. It updates the program in several ways: it sets aside a portion of funding specifically for programs in rural areas and for Native American, Alaska Native, and Native Hawaiian communities; it allows programs to provide meals, help participants sign up for federal benefits like food stamps and childcare assistance, and offer extra support for participants with disabilities. It also requires the federal government to consult regularly with YouthBuild programs when setting performance goals, and directs states to help programs access wage data to track how participants are doing after the program. A new grant program is created to help YouthBuild programs partner with local employers — with preference given to partnerships involving union apprenticeship programs — to connect participants to real jobs. Funding levels for the existing YouthBuild program are set at roughly $159 million to $204 million per year from 2027 through 2032, with an additional $20 million per year available for the new employer partnership grants.
ai_headline: Senate bill reauthorizes and expands YouthBuild program
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

title: DEPORT Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill adds new terrorism-related requirements and consequences to the process by which immigrants become U.S. citizens. Anyone applying for citizenship after the law takes effect would have to sign a sworn statement — under penalty of perjury — confirming they have not been convicted of, or had charges pending for, a specific list of terrorism-related crimes, and that they do not intend to commit such crimes in the future; that same promise would also be added to the citizenship oath. If a naturalized citizen is later convicted of any of those terrorism-related crimes, the government can go to court to strip them of their citizenship (a process called denaturalization), and if that happens, they can be deported and are permanently barred from re-entering the country. People who were naturalized before this law passes are also affected: if they are convicted of one of the listed crimes within a certain number of years after their naturalization, that conviction can be used as evidence that they were never eligible for citizenship in the first place. The bill also makes any non-citizen convicted of these crimes ineligible for virtually all immigration benefits — including green cards, asylum, and temporary protections — with no exceptions or waivers allowed.
ai_headline: Bill ties naturalization to terrorism crime pledges
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: Buy American Seafood Act
ai_summary: This bill would require the federal government to buy only American-caught or American-produced seafood when purchasing fish and other seafood for a wide range of federal food programs — including school lunches and breakfasts, emergency food assistance, military food services, and disaster relief food programs. To qualify as "American," the seafood must be caught in U.S. waters by a U.S.-registered fishing vessel, or raised and processed in the United States through fish farming or domestic processing operations. Agencies could be granted a waiver to buy foreign seafood only if the U.S. supply is not large enough or does not meet required food safety and quality standards — and any such waiver must be publicly reported within 30 days. The bill primarily affects federal agencies that purchase food, seafood suppliers and distributors who sell to the government, and the millions of people who receive food through these federal programs, such as schoolchildren, disaster survivors, and military personnel.
ai_headline: Federal food programs would require U.S.-sourced seafood
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committees on Agriculture, Armed Services, and Transportation and Infrastructure, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Breast Cancer Research Stamp Reauthorization Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill extends the life of a special U.S. Postal Service stamp — called a "semipostal" — that raises money for breast cancer research. A semipostal is a stamp sold at a price higher than its regular postage value, with the extra amount going to a designated cause; in this case, breast cancer research. The bill simply moves the program's expiration date from 2027 to 2037, allowing the stamp to continue being sold for an additional ten years. Anyone who buys and uses this stamp during that period contributes to breast cancer research funding through their everyday postage purchases.
ai_headline: Breast cancer research stamp extended through 2037
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, and in addition to the Committees on Energy and Commerce, and Armed Services, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Ensuring Access to Lower-Cost Medicines for Seniors Act
ai_summary: Starting in 2028, this bill would require Medicare prescription drug plans to automatically include lower-cost generic drugs and biosimilar medicines on their covered drug lists whenever a brand-name version of that drug is already covered and the generic or biosimilar costs less. Plans would also have to place those generics and biosimilars on their own separate, cheaper cost-sharing tier — meaning seniors would pay noticeably less out of pocket for them compared to brand-name drugs, with copayments at least $20 lower than the cheapest brand-name tier. Insurance plans could not make it harder to access a generic or biosimilar than its brand-name counterpart by using tools like prior authorization or step therapy requirements that favor the brand-name version. The bill affects Medicare beneficiaries enrolled in Part D prescription drug plans or Medicare Advantage plans with drug coverage, as well as the insurance companies that run those plans, which would be required to restructure their drug lists and pricing tiers to comply with these new rules.
ai_headline: Medicare Part D plans would be required to cover generics
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

title: Federal Mechanical Insulation Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill requires that when federal buildings undergo energy and water efficiency evaluations, the assessment must now specifically consider whether adding or upgrading **mechanical insulation** — the wrapping or covering placed around pipes, ducts, and other equipment to reduce heat or energy loss — would save energy or water. To qualify, the insulation must meet established industry standards set by a recognized engineering organization and must actually reduce energy loss from the mechanical system it covers. In practical terms, this affects federal agencies that manage government buildings, as well as the contractors and auditors who conduct those efficiency reviews. Ordinary citizens are affected indirectly, since more efficient federal buildings can reduce energy costs paid by taxpayers.
ai_headline: Federal building audits would add mechanical insulation review
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

title: No Big Fossil Bailouts on Your Power Bill Act
ai_summary: This bill places new limits on a federal agency's power to order fossil fuel power plants — like coal or natural gas plants — to keep running during energy emergencies. Under current law, that agency (the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission) can issue emergency orders with few restrictions; this bill would generally prohibit using that authority to delay or prevent a power plant from closing, unless no other option exists to handle the emergency *and* the relevant regional grid operator formally requests the delay in writing. Before issuing or renewing such an emergency order, the Commission would be required to hold a public hearing, consider environmentally friendlier alternatives, check whether the order would raise electricity rates, and consult with state and local regulators. Power companies affected by an order would have to notify their customers in writing — within 60 days — explaining the order's impact and any extra costs for fuel, maintenance, or labor that customers may end up paying. The bill primarily affects the federal Commission, fossil fuel power plant owners, regional electric grid operators, state regulators, and ordinary electricity customers who might see changes in their power bills as a result of these emergency orders.
ai_headline: Senate bill limits FERC emergency orders for fossil fuel plants
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

title: Medication Competition Act
ai_summary: This bill requires the federal health agency (the Department of Health and Human Services) to publicly post clear deadlines showing how long a biological drug — such as a vaccine, insulin, or other complex medicine made from living cells — is protected from competition by similar cheaper versions called biosimilars. Right now, these protection periods (called "exclusivity periods") are not always publicly listed in a timely way, which can make it harder for biosimilar manufacturers to know when they are legally allowed to enter the market. Under this bill, for newly approved drugs, the agency must post that information within 30 days of when the expiration date can first be determined; for drugs already on the market before the law takes effect, the agency has either 30 days from when the date becomes known or up to 2 years to post it. The bill primarily affects drug companies that make biosimilars, companies that make the original biological drugs, and ultimately patients and insurers who may benefit from lower-cost competition once those protection periods expire.
ai_headline: HHS must publish biosimilar exclusivity deadlines online
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

title: Helping Ensure Reliable Opportunities in Child Care for Military Families Act
ai_summary: This bill makes several changes aimed at improving access to child care for military families. It expands who can work at military child development centers by removing a previous service requirement and allowing national service volunteers (such as AmeriCorps participants) trained in education to fill child care roles, and it lets two part-time workers share a single full-time child care position to attract people who cannot work full-time. Workers at these centers could also receive certain on-base benefits — such as access to commissaries, fitness centers, and tuition assistance — to help with hiring and keeping qualified staff. The bill also requires the Defense Department to create a single, standardized data system to track child care capacity, waitlists, staffing gaps, and unmet need across all military installations, and to brief Congress on what that data shows. Finally, the Defense Department must submit reports to Congress on the accuracy of child care waitlist data and on how child care shortages affect military readiness, troop retention, dual-military families, and the employment of military spouses.
ai_headline: DoD child care access expanded for military families
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

title: Special Operations Forces Concealed Carry Act
ai_summary: This bill would allow certain current and former U.S. military special operations personnel to carry a concealed firearm across the country, similar to a right that already exists for retired law enforcement officers under federal law. To qualify, a service member must have served in one of several specific elite units or roles — such as Army Special Forces, Navy SEALs, Army Rangers, Marine Corps Recon or Scout Snipers, or Air Force special operations specialties — and must have held a rank from E-5 (Sergeant/Petty Officer 2nd Class) through the highest officer grades. The carry authority is permanent as long as the person maintains an honorable discharge, stays eligible under federal law to own a firearm, and carries proper identification proving their service; it ends immediately if any of those conditions are no longer met. Unlike retired law enforcement officers, qualifying special operators would not need to pass an annual firearms recertification test. The Departments of Defense and Veterans Affairs would be required to create an ID card program to verify and document eligibility, and the Attorney General would issue guidance to law enforcement agencies explaining how to recognize valid proof of authority to carry.
ai_headline: Special ops veterans would gain nationwide concealed carry rights
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committees on Armed Services, and Veterans' Affairs, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Millionaires Surtax Act
ai_summary: This bill would add an extra 10% tax on top of existing federal income taxes for individuals, estates, and trusts — but only on the portion of their income above a certain threshold. For married couples filing jointly and surviving spouses, the extra tax kicks in on income above $2,000,000; for all other filers (such as single individuals), the threshold is $1,000,000. For example, a single filer with $1,200,000 in qualifying income would owe the extra 10% only on the $200,000 above the $1,000,000 cutoff — not on their entire income. A few specific situations are treated differently: Americans living abroad, nonresident aliens, and certain charitable trusts have special rules that may reduce or eliminate what they owe under this new tax. The changes would take effect starting with the 2027 tax year.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets millionaires with a 10% surtax
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

title: Deescalation Drone Pilot Program Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill directs the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) to create a test program exploring whether small drones equipped only with nonlethal weapons — such as sound emitters, strobe lights, or incapacitating devices designed not to kill — could be safely used by law enforcement during active shooter situations. The goal is to study whether these drones could help protect both officers and the public by allowing police to engage a shooter from a safer distance, particularly in indoor settings. The FAA would work with existing drone testing sites and partner with federal, state, and local law enforcement agencies — including large city and rural police departments — to evaluate how the drones work, how officers should be trained, and what safety rules should apply. Any drones used in the program must be manufactured in the United States. After the program concludes, the FAA must report its findings and then begin creating formal rules so that law enforcement agencies and drone manufacturers can apply for official permission to operate these nonlethal drones.
ai_headline: FAA to test nonlethal police drones for active shooter response
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

title: TRAVEL Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would allow the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) to temporarily send VA doctors to U.S. territories — including Puerto Rico, Guam, the U.S. Virgin Islands, American Samoa, and the Northern Mariana Islands — to provide health care directly to veterans living there. Each assignment could last up to one year, and the VA could send multiple doctors to serve different territories at the same time. These traveling doctors would work at VA facilities or other approved facilities in the territory and would be expected to coordinate with local non-VA medical providers to ensure veterans receive well-rounded care. To encourage doctors to take these assignments, the VA would be required to provide them with a financial bonus — similar to relocation or retention bonuses already offered to other VA physicians — on top of their regular pay. Veterans living in U.S. territories who currently have limited access to VA medical care would be the primary people affected by this bill.
ai_headline: VA would station doctors in U.S. territories for veterans
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Veterans' Affairs.

title: Consumer Protection Remedies Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill strengthens the Federal Trade Commission's (FTC) ability to get money back for consumers who have been harmed by illegal business practices. Right now, due to a 2021 Supreme Court ruling, the FTC has limited power to force companies to return money to cheated consumers; this bill explicitly restores that power by allowing federal courts to order companies to pay restitution (repaying losses to harmed consumers), cancel or rewrite unfair contracts, and give back money or property obtained through wrongdoing. It also allows courts to order "disgorgement," meaning a company can be forced to hand over profits it made illegally, even beyond what it owes directly to consumers — though any restitution already paid counts toward that amount. These remedies can reach back up to 10 years before the FTC files its lawsuit, and that 10-year clock stops running for any period when the company or individual was outside the United States. The bill affects any business or individual that violates laws the FTC enforces — such as laws against fraud, deceptive advertising, or unfair practices — as well as the consumers who were harmed by those violations.
ai_headline: FTC regains power to recover money for defrauded consumers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

title: Supporting VA Families Act
ai_summary: This bill would give employees of the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) an extra four weeks of unpaid time off when they have a new child — whether through birth, adoption, or foster placement. This additional leave must be used within 12 months of the child's birth or placement, and it is on top of any parental leave the employee already receives under existing federal law. It applies to VA employees broadly, including doctors, nurses, and other clinical staff working in the Veterans Health Administration. The leave is unpaid, meaning employees keep their jobs but do not receive their salary during this additional four-week period.
ai_headline: VA workers would get 4 extra weeks of unpaid parental leave
last_action_text: Forwarded by Subcommittee to Full Committee by Voice Vote.

title: For the Fans Act
ai_summary: This bill would require professional sports streaming services — specifically those tied to leagues in baseball, basketball, football, hockey, and soccer — to let paying subscribers watch every game without any blackouts, including geographic blackouts that currently prevent viewers in certain areas from watching local or regional games. In addition, it would require leagues themselves to provide local fans (people living in the same state as a team) with free access to watch all of that team's games, either through a local TV broadcast or a single streaming service, supported by ads instead of subscription fees. These free local streams must be at the best available video quality and delivered through one consistent viewing method. The Federal Trade Commission would be responsible for enforcing the no-blackout rule for streaming services, while the Federal Communications Commission would handle complaints about the free local access requirement, and state attorneys general could also sue on behalf of their residents if either rule is violated. This bill primarily affects professional sports leagues, their affiliated streaming services, and sports fans across the country — particularly those who currently pay for streaming but are blocked from watching certain games, or local fans who cannot afford paid subscriptions.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets sports streaming blackouts
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

title: Safer Prisons Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill doubles the maximum prison sentence and maximum fine that a federal court can impose on anyone convicted of assaulting a correctional officer who works for the federal Bureau of Prisons (BOP). Under current federal law, assaulting a federal officer already carries criminal penalties, but this bill creates a special, higher ceiling specifically for attacks on BOP correctional officers — the guards and staff who work inside federal prisons. For example, if the current maximum sentence for a particular type of assault is 8 years, the new maximum for that same assault committed against a BOP officer would become 16 years. The bill affects anyone who physically attacks a federal prison employee in their official capacity, and it applies to federal prosecutors and judges who handle those cases, since it changes the upper limits of punishment they can consider.
ai_headline: Senate bill doubles penalties for BOP officer assaults
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

title: Reforming Disaster Recovery Act
ai_summary: This bill creates a permanent, dedicated government fund — called the Long-Term Disaster Recovery Fund — that the Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) can draw from to send money to states, cities, and Native American tribes after major disasters, helping them rebuild housing, restore infrastructure, revitalize local economies, and reduce the risk of future disaster damage. At least 70 percent of the money must be spent on helping low- and moderate-income people, and up to 30 percent of each grant can go toward making communities more resilient against future disasters. The bill also creates a new office within HUD specifically dedicated to disaster preparedness and recovery, sets up rules for how quickly money must be allocated after a disaster is declared, and requires grantees to develop public plans explaining how they will spend the funds. To prevent waste and overlap, the bill establishes data-sharing arrangements between HUD, FEMA, and the Small Business Administration so that disaster survivors don't receive duplicate payments from multiple programs. The people most directly affected are low- and moderate-income households, renters, homeowners, and communities — especially the most heavily damaged ones — recovering from presidentially declared major disasters.
ai_headline: HUD would gain a permanent disaster recovery fund
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Financial Services, and in addition to the Committee on Appropriations, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Protecting Families from Fertility Fraud Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill makes it a federal crime for anyone — such as a fertility doctor or clinic worker — to knowingly lie about whose DNA (sperm, eggs, or embryos) is used during fertility treatments like IVF or artificial insemination. For example, a doctor who secretly uses his own sperm instead of the agreed-upon donor's without telling the patient would be committing a federal offense. Someone convicted of this crime could face a fine, up to 10 years in prison, or both. The bill also includes a special rule on timing: if DNA testing later reveals who committed the fraud, prosecutors have 10 years from the date that person is identified to bring charges, even if the normal time limit for prosecution has already passed. This primarily affects fertility healthcare providers and patients who undergo assisted reproductive procedures anywhere in the United States or involving interstate travel or commerce.
ai_headline: Bill targets DNA fraud in fertility treatments
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: A bill to prohibit the Export-Import Bank of the United States from providing financing to persons with seriously delinquent tax debt.
ai_summary: This bill would prevent the Export-Import Bank — a federal agency that helps finance American exports — from giving loans, loan guarantees, or other financial support to any person or business that owes seriously overdue federal taxes. It also blocks financing for any project if *any* participant in that project has such a tax debt. The restriction would not apply in cases where the person is actively repaying their tax debt under a payment plan, has a legal challenge or appeal in progress, or has a wage garnishment already in place to collect the debt. The President could override this ban in specific situations, but only if he determines there are urgent circumstances affecting U.S. interests and reports that reasoning to Congress within 30 days. This rule would affect any individual, company, or other participant seeking Export-Import Bank financing who has an unpaid federal tax bill that the IRS has already assessed and could legally collect.
ai_headline: Bill bars Ex-Im Bank aid to tax delinquent businesses
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

title: To amend title 28, United States Code, to prohibit Presidents and Vice Presidents from receiving damages payments from the United States, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would bar the sitting President, sitting Vice President, their spouses and dependent children, and certain related legal entities from collecting any money from the federal government through settlements, consent decrees, or administrative agreements — and it would also prevent them from even filing such claims while they are in office. If one of those individuals still wants to sue the government in court for actual losses they suffered, they can only do so under strict conditions: an independent lawyer, chosen by the court and removable only by the court, must be appointed to represent the government's side, and all court filings and audio from hearings must be posted online for the public at no charge. Once a President or Vice President leaves office, they may file claims again, but only if career employees — not anyone appointed by the current President — handle the review, and any resulting payments or agreements must be publicly disclosed in the Federal Register within seven days and reported to Congress. Anyone who willfully breaks these rules faces civil fines, repayment of any money received, and possible prison time.
ai_headline: Bill bars presidents from settling claims against U.S. while in office
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: ENDS Chinese Vapes Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill creates new financial penalties for anyone who illegally imports vaping products (e-cigarettes, pods, cartridges, or similar devices) into the United States without FDA approval. Depending on whether the smuggling was intentional fraud, serious carelessness, or ordinary carelessness, the penalty per individual vaping device can be up to $5,000, $1,000, or $500 respectively — and those amounts can be multiplied if the importer routed the shipment through another country to hide its origin, or if the same importer has been caught doing this before within three years. The total penalty for any single shipment, however, cannot exceed ten times the estimated U.S. retail value of that shipment. The bill primarily affects importers, distributors, and businesses that bring unauthorized vaping products into the country, and it does not prevent other federal agencies like the FDA or Department of Justice from also pursuing their own separate legal actions against violators.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets illegal vaping imports with steep fines
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

title: Stop CHEATERS Act
ai_summary: This bill would give the IRS large amounts of additional funding over six years (2026–2031) to strengthen tax enforcement, improve taxpayer services, and overhaul its technology systems. The money is divided into four areas: pursuing unpaid taxes and conducting audits and criminal investigations; helping taxpayers file and understand their obligations; updating outdated IRS computer systems to better detect fraud; and modernizing the agency's core business software. The bill specifically directs the IRS to focus more of its auditing and enforcement efforts on high-income individuals and large corporations, rather than ordinary wage earners. The IRS Commissioner would also be required to report to Congress every two years on the agency's progress toward those goals, and an independent watchdog — the Treasury Inspector General — would separately review and evaluate that progress.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets IRS funding for wealthy audits
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

title: SAFER Transport Act
ai_summary: This bill creates new rules and government actions aimed at reducing fraud and theft in the freight trucking industry. It sets up an advisory committee of trucking, railroad, port, insurance, and law enforcement representatives to recommend ways the government can better fight cargo fraud and theft, and requires the Department of Transportation and the Department of Justice to formally agree on how they will share information about freight crimes. The bill phases out a separate trucking identification number (MC number) over five years so that all truckers, freight brokers, and freight forwarders use a single government ID number, and it allows the government to deny or revoke registration from anyone convicted of serious crimes like theft, trafficking, or smuggling. It also requires states to verify that non-citizen applicants for commercial driver's licenses are legally authorized to work in the U.S., bans foreign-based trucking companies and unauthorized workers from hauling cargo entirely within the United States, requires overseas dispatch services to register as brokers, and makes it a federal crime — punishable by up to five years in prison — to submit false information to obtain trucking authority. These changes primarily affect trucking companies, freight brokers, cargo shippers, commercial driver's license applicants, foreign dispatch services, and the federal and state agencies that oversee them.
ai_headline: Bill targets fraud and theft in freight trucking industry
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure, and in addition to the Committees on the Judiciary, and Homeland Security, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Every Dollar Counts Act of 2026
ai_summary: If you currently have health insurance and choose to buy a prescription drug out of pocket — meaning you pay the full price yourself without using your insurance benefits — the money you spend would still count toward your deductible and your annual out-of-pocket maximum, just as if you had used your insurance. This rule would apply to people covered by employer-sponsored health plans as well as those with individual health insurance. In practice, this could help people reach their deductible or out-of-pocket cap faster in years when they pay for certain drugs on their own, for example when a drug is cheaper to buy directly than through their plan. The bill also adjusts two drug-pricing calculations used in Medicaid — "best price" and "average manufacturer price" — so that discounts given when someone buys a drug without using any insurance or government health program are not factored into those calculations. These changes would take effect for insurance plan years starting on or after January 1, 2027.
ai_headline: Out-of-pocket drug costs would count toward insurance deductibles
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committees on Education and Workforce, and Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Department of Education relating to "William D. Ford Federal Direct Loan (Direct Loan) Program".
ai_summary: Congress is voting to cancel a rule that the Department of Education published on October 31, 2025, about the William D. Ford Federal Direct Loan program, which is the main federal student loan program used by millions of Americans. If this joint resolution passes both chambers and is signed into law, that rule would be wiped out and treated as if it never existed. The bill does not spell out what the rule itself contains — it simply disapproves the entire rule and strips it of any legal effect. People most directly affected would be anyone currently repaying, applying for, or managing federal Direct Loans, since the rule being cancelled would no longer apply to them.
ai_headline: Senate moves to void Education Dept. direct loan rule
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

title: National Veterans Strategy Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would require the President to create a formal definition of what it means for a veteran to succeed after military service, measured across areas like physical and mental health, finances, education, family life, and civic participation. Working with a wide range of partners — including federal agencies, state and local governments, tribal organizations, veterans groups, colleges, businesses, and nonprofits — the President would also be required to develop a written national plan every four years explaining how all those organizations should work together to help veterans reach those goals. The plan must account for the different needs of veterans across age, race, gender, disability status, geography, and other factors, and federal agencies would be required to align their own plans and budgets with it. Every year, the President would have to report to Congress on how well the plan is working, including what is being spent and what barriers exist. Congress would have the power to reject any submitted plan within 60 days through a formal vote, and the bill explicitly states that it cannot be used to cut or eliminate any benefits or programs already guaranteed by law.
ai_headline: Bill would set a national plan for veteran success
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Veterans' Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on Rules, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Workforce Recovery and Resilience Act
ai_summary: This bill would do two main things to help communities hit hard by substance use disorders (such as opioid addiction). First, it requires the federal Department of Labor to gather and share up-to-date, research-backed information with states and local governments about the best ways to address how widespread drug addiction affects local workers and economies, updating that information every year. Second, it creates a new category of federal grants that local areas can apply for to fund job training and employment programs connected to preventing and treating substance use disorders — covering things like addiction treatment, mental health services, and pain management. People who could benefit from those grants include laid-off workers, the long-term unemployed, people whose jobs were disrupted by widespread substance use in their community, and healthcare workers (current or aspiring) who work in addiction or mental health treatment fields.
ai_headline: Labor Dept. to fund addiction workforce recovery grants
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

title: Parental Bereavement Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would allow employees to take unpaid, job-protected leave from work after the death of a son or daughter of any age. Currently, the Family and Medical Leave Act (FMLA) already covers leave for things like a child's serious illness, but it does not specifically guarantee leave when a child dies — this bill would add that as a covered reason. The leave must be taken within 12 months of the child's death, and while it generally must be taken all at once rather than in scattered days, an employee and employer can mutually agree to a different schedule. This protection would apply both to private-sector employees covered by the FMLA and to federal government workers, and it also allows employees to substitute any accrued paid leave (such as vacation or sick time) for the unpaid leave if they choose. Employers would be permitted to ask for documentation supporting the leave request, subject to any rules the relevant federal agency sets.
ai_headline: Bill expands FMLA to cover leave after a child's death
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committees on Oversight and Government Reform, and House Administration, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Further Additional Continuing Appropriations Act, 2026
ai_summary: This law provides the federal government's funding for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2026, focusing primarily on the Department of Homeland Security and, through a second portion of the bill, continuing funding for the rest of the federal government. It sets specific dollar amounts for agencies including U.S. Customs and Border Protection, the Transportation Security Administration, the Coast Guard, the U.S. Secret Service, the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, and the Federal Emergency Management Agency, covering their day-to-day operations, equipment purchases, construction, and grants to states and localities. Ordinary Americans are affected in a wide range of ways — for example, through TSA screening at airports, FEMA disaster relief and flood insurance, homeland security grants to local police and fire departments, nonprofit security grants, and Coast Guard operations. The law also sets rules on how the money can be spent, such as prohibiting new land border crossing fees, requiring body cameras for certain immigration enforcement officers, and directing oversight of detention facilities.
ai_headline: Federal agencies receive FY2026 homeland security funds
last_action_text: Message on Senate action sent to the House.

title: Critical Undersea Infrastructure Resilience Initiative Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the U.S. government to take a series of steps to protect the underwater cables and pipelines near Taiwan that carry internet, phone, and energy services, which Congress says have been repeatedly cut or damaged by ships linked to China. Within about a year of passage, the Secretary of State — working with the Defense, Homeland Security, and Coast Guard — must launch a formal program to improve real-time monitoring of those cables, set up quick-repair systems with regional allies, increase naval and coast guard patrols around Taiwan, and encourage physical strengthening of the cables themselves, with up to $20 million per year authorized from 2027 through 2032 for the rapid-response work. The bill also requires the President to impose financial sanctions (freezing U.S.-held assets) and visa bans on any foreign individual, company, or ship crew found responsible for sabotaging undersea infrastructure important to Taiwan or other U.S. allies. Additionally, it establishes a senior White House-led planning group to prepare the U.S. government for possible crises in the Taiwan Strait — such as a blockade or military conflict — and requires that group to report to Congress annually for ten years. The bill most directly affects U.S. federal agencies, Taiwan, regional allies, and any foreign actors who damage or help damage critical undersea infrastructure.
ai_headline: Bill targets sabotage of undersea cables near Taiwan
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Fair Credit Reporting Reseller Accuracy Act
ai_summary: This bill adds a new rule for **resellers** — companies that buy credit report information from major credit bureaus and then sell or pass it along to lenders, landlords, employers, or other businesses. Under the new rule, before a reseller forwards someone's credit information to whoever ordered it, the reseller must take reasonable steps to make sure that information is as accurate as possible. However, the bill also protects resellers from being sued if they accurately passed along information exactly as they received it from the original credit bureau — meaning the responsibility for bad data stays with the source, not the middleman. This change would primarily affect reseller companies in the credit reporting industry, as well as everyday consumers whose credit information moves through those companies before reaching a lender or other decision-maker.
ai_headline: Bill targets accuracy rules for credit report resellers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

title: Restoring College Access and Affordability Act
ai_summary: This bill would undo a set of recent changes to federal student aid programs that were enacted as part of a 2025 budget law, essentially restoring the rules that existed before those changes took effect. Specifically, it would reverse cuts to how much students can borrow in federal loans, reverse changes to repayment plans and deferment options, reverse restrictions on Public Service Loan Forgiveness, and restore the prior rules for Pell Grant eligibility and how Pell Grants interact with other aid — all of which would affect current and future college students who rely on federal financial assistance. The bill also cancels delays that had been placed on two borrower-protection rules: one allowing students to seek loan cancellation if their school misled them, and another allowing automatic loan discharge if their school closed. Additionally, the bill expands an existing law that can cut off federal financial aid to educational programs whose graduates consistently earn too little to repay their loans, applying that rule more broadly across certificate, associate's, bachelor's, and graduate programs. Finally, it fixes the endowment tax that wealthy private universities pay — locking it at a flat rate of 1.4 percent and removing a tiered rate structure that had been added by the 2025 law.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets 2025 cuts to federal student aid
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

title: Workforce Investments Accountability Act
ai_summary: This bill changes the rules for how federal job training programs are measured, reported on, and funded under the main federal workforce law. It updates the yardsticks used to judge whether job training programs are succeeding — for example, by tracking whether workers stay employed longer after leaving a program and by counting how many participants complete hands-on training like apprenticeships or on-the-job training. It strengthens consequences for states and local areas whose programs fall short of those targets, including funding reductions, while rewarding states that show the most improvement by directing extra money their way. It also requires that at least half of certain local job training funds be spent directly on actual training services — such as individual training accounts or training contracts — rather than on other program costs. These changes primarily affect state and local workforce agencies, job training providers, and the workers and jobseekers who use federally funded employment and training programs.
ai_headline: Bill tightens job training standards and funding rules
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

title: Fertilizer Transparency Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would require fertilizer manufacturers and wholesalers to report their prices and sales quantities to the U.S. Department of Agriculture every week, so that the government can regularly publish that information to the public. The goal is to give farmers and other market participants clearer, more timely information about what fertilizer actually costs, so they can make better buying decisions. Cooperatives and retailers (unless a retailer is also a manufacturer) would be exempt from the mandatory reporting but could choose to share their price and quantity information voluntarily. The Department of Agriculture would also run a weekly retail price survey and publish all of this data through an easy-to-access online dashboard, while keeping individual companies' confidential business details private. The bill does not change any existing antitrust laws.
ai_headline: USDA would track weekly fertilizer prices under new bill
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

title: Permanent Housing Affordability Act
ai_summary: This bill creates several government programs to make homeownership permanently affordable for low- and moderate-income people by supporting "shared equity" housing models — arrangements where homes are sold at below-market prices with legal agreements (like ground leases or deed restrictions) requiring them to stay affordable for at least 99 years whenever they are resold. It sets up a fund at the Treasury Department to give grants to state agencies and certain nonprofit lenders, who would then make low-interest construction loans (capped at 3% interest) to nonprofits and local governments to build or renovate homes that are then sold only to buyers earning up to 120% of the local median income. A separate pilot grant program through the Department of Housing and Urban Development would help eligible nonprofits and local governments buy vacant land or existing properties to develop and sell as permanently affordable homes, primarily to households earning up to 80% of the local median income (or 120% in rural areas). The bill also directs the federal government to make surplus federal properties available to community land trusts and similar organizations at a 75% discount off market value, and launches public education and research programs to raise awareness of shared equity homeownership among potential buyers, lenders, and real estate professionals. The people most directly affected are lower- and moderate-income prospective homebuyers, nonprofit housing organizations, community land trusts, local governments, and lenders who work in affordable housing.
ai_headline: Bill would fund permanently affordable homeownership programs
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Financial Services, and in addition to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Save Struggling Hospitals Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how Medicare calculates payments to hospitals located in areas where wages are relatively low. Specifically, hospitals whose local wage index score falls below the 25th percentile (meaning they are among the lower-paid quarter of hospitals) would receive a boost — their wage index score would be raised halfway toward the 25th percentile level, resulting in higher Medicare reimbursement payments. The adjustment would apply retroactively starting October 1, 2019, and would be carried out in a way that keeps total Medicare spending the same overall (budget neutral), meaning some other hospitals would see slight reductions to offset the increases. To protect all hospitals from steep cuts, the bill sets guardrails: hospitals below the 75th percentile cannot have their scores reduced to fund the adjustment, and no hospital's score can drop below 95% of what it was the prior year. In short, this primarily helps lower-wage, often rural or underserved hospitals receive more Medicare money, while limiting how much higher-wage hospitals can be affected.
ai_headline: Medicare wage index boost proposed for low-pay hospitals
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

title: Chesapeake Bay Watershed Advancement for Training, Education, Restoration, and Science (WATERS) Act
ai_summary: This bill renews and updates the federal government office within the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) that focuses on the Chesapeake Bay — the large estuary shared by Virginia, Maryland, and several surrounding states. The office would be led by a Director with hands-on experience in the Bay's research or resource management, and would be responsible for scientific monitoring of water quality and wildlife, coordinating with state and federal partners, and working with a network of buoys that track weather and ecological conditions in the Bay. The bill also authorizes two new formal programs: one to fund education and teacher training about the Bay for K-12 students and schools, and another to support the protection and restoration of important species and habitats such as oysters, blue crabs, striped bass, underwater grasses, and fish communities. People most directly affected include researchers, educators, fishermen, coastal communities, and state and local agencies around the Chesapeake Bay watershed, as well as students and teachers in the region whose environmental education programs could receive grant funding under the bill.
ai_headline: NOAA's Chesapeake Bay Office renewed with new education programs
last_action_text: Subcommittee Hearings Held

title: American Seafood Competitiveness Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill opens up U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) loan and grant programs — which have historically been available only to farmers and ranchers — to commercial fishermen and fish processors as well. Under the bill, people who catch fish for a living or who operate fish processing businesses could apply for USDA loans to buy fishing permits, purchase or upgrade fishing vessels, or acquire and improve fish processing facilities, and could also use operating loans to cover the day-to-day costs of running those operations. Wild-caught seafood would also become eligible under a USDA program that promotes local and regional food markets, and the agency would have the flexibility to waive or reduce matching-fund requirements for fishing and processing businesses applying for those marketing grants. Separately, the bill allows Farm Credit banks and production credit associations — a network of lenders that provide financing to agricultural producers — to extend credit to businesses that supply services directly to fish and seafood harvesters. Within one year of passage, the USDA would be required to take steps to bring the fishing industry into these programs, including reaching out to fishermen, training agency staff, and coordinating with other federal and state agencies.
ai_headline: USDA loans would expand to cover commercial fishing
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

title: Permanent Housing Affordability Act
ai_summary: This bill creates several federal programs to help make homeownership permanently affordable for low- and moderate-income people by supporting "shared equity" models — arrangements where a nonprofit or government entity keeps homes affordable long-term by limiting how much a seller can profit when reselling. It sets up a $100 million loan fund managed by the Treasury Department that channels low-interest construction loans (at no more than 3% interest) to local nonprofits and similar organizations to build or fix up homes that must be sold to buyers earning no more than 120% of the local median income. A separate pilot grant program, authorized at $100 million per year for five years through the Department of Housing and Urban Development, lets those same kinds of organizations buy land or existing properties to develop affordable homes, primarily for buyers earning up to 80% of the local median income (or 120% in rural areas). The bill also allows the federal government to transfer surplus federal land to community land trusts or similar organizations at a 75% discount off market value. Finally, it directs the government to conduct research, public education campaigns, and outreach to homebuyers, lenders, builders, and real estate professionals about how these shared equity homeownership models work.
ai_headline: Senate bill backs shared equity homeownership funds
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

title: Plug Offshore Wells Act
ai_summary: Every year, the Secretary of the Interior would be required to compile and publish a public report tracking the cleanup and shutdown of old offshore oil and gas wells, platforms, and pipelines in U.S. waters. The report would cover how many of these structures were supposed to be shut down, how many actually were, how many missed their required deadlines, and what enforcement actions — such as fines or penalties — were taken against companies that didn't comply. It would also track details about structures that were left in place underwater versus those that were physically removed. This bill primarily affects the Department of the Interior, the Bureau of Safety and Environmental Enforcement, and the oil and gas companies that own or operate offshore drilling equipment — but the resulting reports would be freely available to any member of the public on the department's website.
ai_headline: Interior Dept. must report yearly on offshore well cleanups
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

title: Plug Offshore Wells Act
ai_summary: This bill requires the Secretary of the Interior to produce a public annual report on the progress of shutting down and cleaning up old offshore oil and gas wells, platforms, and pipelines in U.S. waters. The report must track how many companies were required to submit decommissioning applications, how many actually did, and how many wells, platforms, and pipelines missed their legally required deadlines for being closed down or removed.
