It must also cover how many structures were approved to be left in place on the ocean floor rather than physically removed, and the total length of pipelines either left in place or removed. The report must include information on any enforcement actions taken against companies that failed to meet their decommissioning obligations, such as fines or bans from future offshore operations. This bill primarily affects the Department of the Interior, offshore oil and gas companies operating in U.S. waters, and members of the public or policymakers who want to track whether these companies are properly cleaning up their old offshore infrastructure.
ai_headline: Interior Dept. must report on offshore well cleanup progress
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

title: Providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Department of Labor relating to the Adverse Effect Wage Rate.
ai_summary: This resolution would cancel a Department of Labor rule that was published in October 2025 and deals with how wages are set for temporary foreign farmworkers who come to the U.S. on H-2A visas for non-ranch jobs (such as crop harvesting). The rule being canceled established a method for calculating the "Adverse Effect Wage Rate," which is the minimum hourly pay that employers must offer H-2A workers to avoid driving down wages for American workers doing similar jobs. If this resolution passes, that wage-setting rule would be wiped out and treated as if it never took effect. This would primarily affect agricultural employers who hire H-2A temporary workers, the foreign workers themselves, and U.S. farmworkers whose wages are protected by those minimum pay requirements.
ai_headline: House moves to repeal DOL's H-2A farmworker wage rule
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: INSULIN Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would limit how much people with private health insurance pay out of pocket for insulin to no more than $35 per month's supply, with no deductible required, starting in 2027 — and from 2028 onward, that cap could be even lower if the plan's actual negotiated price works out to less. It also requires that any rebates or discounts that drug manufacturers give to pharmacy benefit managers or insurers related to insulin must be passed back entirely to the health plan, rather than kept by middlemen. For people who are uninsured, the bill would create a 5-year grant program giving 10 states up to $100 million total to help uninsured people access insulin for no more than $35 per month, and it would fund a resource center and 24/7 hotline to connect uninsured individuals with assistance programs. Finally, the bill includes measures to make it harder to use legal petitions to delay the approval of lower-cost generic or biosimilar versions of insulin, and it allows the FDA to fast-track review of biosimilar insulin products when there are fewer than three competing biosimilars on the market.
ai_headline: Senate bill caps insulin costs at $35 for insured patients
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

title: AI/AN CAPTA
ai_summary: This bill changes how federal money for child abuse prevention and treatment programs is shared, specifically to direct more funding to American Indian and Alaska Native communities. Currently, a small slice of these funds (1 percent) is set aside for migrant programs, but nothing is specifically reserved for tribal communities. Under this bill, 5 percent of the available funding would be set aside for Indian Tribes and Tribal organizations, while the existing 1 percent for migrant programs would be kept. The bill also explicitly adds Indian Tribes and Tribal organizations to the list of groups that must receive an equitable share of assistance under the law. The people most directly affected are American Indian and Alaska Native children and families, as well as the tribal governments and organizations that run child abuse prevention and treatment services in their communities.
ai_headline: Bill sets aside 5% of child abuse funds for tribal groups
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

title: SPARK Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the Small Business Administration (SBA) to create two new programs aimed at helping startups and small businesses in communities that typically struggle to access business support and funding — including low-income neighborhoods, rural areas, and communities with large minority populations. The first program would allow the SBA to provide funding agreements (at least $500,000 per year each) to organizations like nonprofits, community banks, credit unions, and community colleges that run incubators or accelerators — programs that mentor and support new and growing businesses — as long as those organizations serve groups such as women, minorities, veterans, people with disabilities, formerly incarcerated individuals, and rural entrepreneurs. The second program would allow those same types of organizations to receive up to $1,000,000 per year from the SBA and use that money to make small grants (capped at $20,000 per business) or low-interest loans directly to qualifying small businesses owned by people from those underserved groups or located in economically distressed areas, all at no fee to the businesses receiving help. Both programs include requirements for annual reporting to Congress, financial oversight, privacy protections for participants, and training support for the organizations running the programs.
ai_headline: SBA would fund incubators serving underserved entrepreneurs
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Small Business.

title: To direct the removal of United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Republic of Cuba that have not been authorized by Congress.
ai_summary: This resolution would require the President to pull U.S. military forces out of any fighting involving Cuba unless Congress has formally declared war on Cuba or passed a specific law approving military action there. It also explicitly states that using the U.S. Coast Guard or other military forces to blockade or quarantine Cuba counts as the kind of military action that requires Congressional approval. The resolution would affect the President's ability to conduct military operations against or inside Cuba without first getting permission from Congress. There is one exception built in: nothing in the resolution would stop the U.S. from defending itself against an actual or imminent attack, or from carrying out lawful drug-interdiction operations.
ai_headline: Bill would require Congress to approve Cuba military action
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

title: AI/AN CAPTA
ai_summary: This bill changes how federal money for child abuse prevention and treatment programs is shared, specifically to direct more funding to American Indian and Alaska Native communities. Under current law, a small share of these funds must go to programs serving migrant families, but Native tribes and tribal organizations are not specifically guaranteed their own dedicated portion. This bill fixes that by requiring that 5 percent of available funds be set aside for Indian Tribes and tribal organizations, while keeping 1 percent for migrant programs. It also adds Indian Tribes and tribal organizations by name to the list of groups that must receive an equitable share of federal child abuse prevention assistance. The people most directly affected are children and families in Native American and Alaska Native communities, as well as the tribal governments and organizations that run child welfare and abuse prevention programs serving those communities.
ai_headline: Tribal groups gain dedicated child abuse prevention funding
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

title: FAIC Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how Medicare pays hospitals for certain newer cancer drugs and biologics given in outpatient settings. Right now, payment for some of these treatments is "bundled" or folded into a single lump payment for a broader outpatient visit or procedure, which can leave hospitals underpaid for expensive drugs. Under this bill, if a cancer treatment was FDA-approved on or after January 1, 2008, and its estimated daily cost reaches or exceeds $350 (a threshold that would rise each year with inflation), Medicare must pay for it separately rather than bundling it into the overall visit payment. The separate payment would generally be based on the drug's average sales price, similar to how Medicare already pays for other outpatient drugs. The bill primarily affects hospitals and other outpatient providers that administer these cancer treatments, as well as Medicare patients who may otherwise lose access to costly newer therapies if providers cannot cover their costs under bundled payment rates.
ai_headline: Medicare would pay separately for newer outpatient cancer drugs
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Feeding Families Not Fear Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would undo two things that a previously enacted law (referred to in the bill as the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act") put in place. First, it would cancel the extra funding that law gave to Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), taking back any of that money that has not yet been spent. Second, it would restore food stamp (SNAP) benefits to their earlier, higher levels by wiping out the portion of that prior law that reduced those benefits. The people most directly affected are SNAP recipients — low-income individuals and families who rely on food assistance — who would see their benefit amounts return to what they were before the cuts, as well as the federal immigration enforcement agency, which would lose the additional funding it had received.
ai_headline: Bill targets ICE funds and restores SNAP benefit levels
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Agriculture, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: GUARD VA Benefits Act
ai_summary: This bill restores a federal criminal penalty for anyone who charges a veteran an unauthorized fee to help file a VA benefits claim. Under current law, certain accredited attorneys and claims agents are allowed to charge fees, but anyone else — such as unaccredited individuals or companies — is prohibited from doing so. If this bill becomes law, anyone who solicits, charges, or collects an unauthorized fee related to preparing or filing a VA benefits claim could be fined under federal law. The people most affected are veterans seeking benefits help and any individuals or businesses that offer claims assistance services without proper government accreditation.
ai_headline: Bill would restore fines for unauthorized VA claims fees
last_action_text: Committee Hearings Held

title: Save the World Cup Act
ai_summary: This bill would ban the use of federal money by the Department of Homeland Security or the Department of Justice to conduct civil immigration enforcement — such as detaining or arresting people for immigration violations — within one mile of any 2026 FIFA World Cup soccer match or official Fan Festival held in the United States. The restriction would apply to the areas immediately surrounding those events, meaning immigration agents could not use federal funds to carry out those activities in that zone during those events. There are four exceptions where enforcement would still be allowed: if there is an immediate threat of death, violence, or physical harm to someone (including terrorism); an imminent threat to national security; the active pursuit or arrest of someone posing an immediate public safety danger; or the risk that evidence in an ongoing criminal case is about to be destroyed. This bill would primarily affect federal immigration enforcement agencies and, indirectly, the large number of fans — including foreign nationals — expected to attend World Cup matches and Fan Festivals across the country.
ai_headline: Bill would bar DHS immigration arrests near World Cup sites
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: Energy Bills Relief Act
ai_summary: This bill is a broad package of energy policy changes aimed at lowering electricity and heating costs for American households, particularly lower-income families. It would restore clean energy tax credits that were recently rolled back, expand and increase funding for programs that help low-income households pay their heating and cooling bills, and create rebates for energy-saving home improvements like reflective roofing and weatherization upgrades. It would also require that wind, solar, and energy storage projects go through the same federal permitting process as oil, gas, and coal projects — no easier, but no harder — and would invest in modernizing the electricity grid, including faster connections for new power generators and stronger transmission lines. The bill affects a wide range of people and organizations: low-income families who rely on heating and cooling assistance, homeowners seeking energy efficiency help, energy companies and utilities, federal agencies overseeing energy and environmental rules, and communities near public lands and waters where energy projects are developed.
ai_headline: Bill would cut home energy costs and restore clean-energy tax credits
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committees on Agriculture, Ways and Means, Natural Resources, Financial Services, Transportation and Infrastructure, Education and Workforce, Oversight and Government Reform, and Science, Space, and Technology, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act
ai_summary: This wide-ranging bill is designed to increase the supply and affordability of housing across the United States through dozens of separate measures affecting homebuyers, renters, landlords, builders, lenders, and government agencies at all levels. On the homeownership side, it creates pilot programs to make small mortgages (under $100,000) more accessible, strengthens housing counseling for people at risk of foreclosure, and takes steps to make it easier for lenders — especially community banks and credit unions — to offer home loans. For renters and low-income households, it funds home repair grants for low-income homeowners and small landlords, supports affordable housing construction, and launches a pilot program to install temperature sensors in federally assisted rental units to help ensure safe living conditions. For builders and local governments, it directs federal agencies to publish guidelines on zoning reforms (such as reducing parking requirements and allowing more types of housing), streamlines environmental reviews for certain rural housing projects, and encourages repurposing vacant buildings and publicly owned land into housing. The bill also addresses manufactured and modular housing, veteran homelessness, oversight of federal housing programs, and banking rules — making it one of the broadest federal housing policy packages in recent memory.
ai_headline: Bill targets housing costs with zoning and loan reforms
last_action_text: Message on Senate action sent to the House.

title: State Veterans Homes Inspection Simplification Act
ai_summary: This bill would allow nursing homes run by states specifically for veterans — called State Veterans Homes — to skip a separate federal inspection process for Medicare and Medicaid if they have already been inspected and certified by the Department of Veterans Affairs, as long as the VA's inspection standards meet or exceed federal Medicare requirements. Right now, these facilities can face overlapping inspections from both the VA and federal Medicare/Medicaid authorities, and this bill would let a passing VA inspection count for both, reducing that duplication. The federal government would still be able to investigate complaints, impose penalties, or pull a facility's "deemed compliant" status if problems are found, and inspection results would still be made available to the public online. This affects veterans living in state-run veterans nursing homes, the states that operate those homes, the VA, and the federal agency that oversees Medicare and Medicaid. An independent government watchdog would be required to review how well the new system is working after three years and report back to Congress.
ai_headline: VA inspections would satisfy Medicare rules at state veterans homes
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.

title: Clarence Mitchell, Jr. Statue Act
ai_summary: This bill directs a congressional committee — the Joint Committee of Congress on the Library — to obtain a statue of Clarence Mitchell, Jr. and place it permanently in the U.S. Capitol building, with two years to arrange that acquisition. Clarence Mitchell, Jr. was a Baltimore-born civil rights leader who directed the NAACP's Washington office for nearly three decades and played a major role in the passage of landmark civil rights legislation in the 1950s, 1960s, and 1970s; he received the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1980. The bill authorizes whatever federal funding is needed to cover the cost of the statue. It primarily affects the congressional and Capitol administrative officials responsible for carrying out the acquisition and installation, while the statue itself would be visible to the general public who visit the Capitol.
ai_headline: Capitol to receive statue of civil rights leader Clarence Mitchell
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.

title: ACTION for National Service Act
ai_summary: This bill converts the existing Corporation for National and Community Service — the federal body that oversees AmeriCorps and related volunteer programs — into a new executive agency called the AmeriCorps Administration, led by a Director with cabinet-level rank rather than a corporate board structure. It significantly expands educational awards for people who complete national service, setting the award amount equal to twice the average annual in-state tuition at a public four-year college, and makes both those awards and volunteers' living allowances tax-free. The bill aims to grow national service participation to at least one million people per year within ten years, and it creates an outreach program to notify every American between ages 17 and 30 about opportunities to serve in AmeriCorps, the Peace Corps, or the military. It also establishes a new nonprofit National Service Foundation to accept private donations in support of service programs. These changes would primarily affect current and future AmeriCorps and VISTA volunteers, nonprofit organizations that run service programs, and young Americans who might be recruited into national service.
ai_headline: Senate bill would expand AmeriCorps awards and reach
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance. (Sponsor introductory remarks on measure: CR S1048-1049)

title: ACTION for National Service Act
ai_summary: This bill would transform AmeriCorps — the federal agency that runs national volunteer and community service programs — from a government corporation into a full executive department led by a Director (similar in rank to a Cabinet secretary), renaming it the "AmeriCorps Administration." It would increase the education awards that volunteers earn for their service, setting them equal to twice the average annual in-state tuition at public four-year colleges, and would make both those education awards and the living allowances paid to participants tax-free. The bill also sets a goal of expanding national service opportunities to reach at least one million participants per year within ten years, and creates an outreach program to contact every American between ages 17 and 30 to inform them about service opportunities. Additionally, it establishes a new private nonprofit National Service Foundation to accept donations supporting the agency's mission, and creates an interagency working group to study whether national service veterans should receive preference in federal hiring. The bill primarily affects current and future AmeriCorps volunteers and participants in related programs like VISTA and the National Senior Service Corps, as well as the organizations and communities they serve.
ai_headline: Bill would expand AmeriCorps and raise service awards
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Justice for Hind Rajab Act
ai_summary: This bill responds to the killing of a 5-year-old girl named Hind Rajab, six of her family members, and two paramedics in Gaza City on January 29, 2024, and requires specific U.S. government actions to investigate what happened. Within 45 days of becoming law, the Secretary of State must submit a detailed report to Congress covering what U.S. officials know about the attack — including whether the weapons used were supplied by the United States, whether any U.S. citizens were among the soldiers involved, and whether those soldiers received U.S. training. If credible information suggests the attack may have been a war crime and involved U.S. weapons, citizens, or U.S.-trained personnel, the Secretary of State must refer that information to the Attorney General, who must then certify that the Justice Department will review it and potentially open a criminal investigation and prosecution under existing U.S. war crimes law. The bill also expresses Congress's view that Israel should compensate the victims' families, and that the U.S. should do the same if American citizens serving in the Israeli military are found to have been involved.
ai_headline: Bill would probe U.S. role in Gaza girl's killing
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: FOOD for Health Act
ai_summary: This bill would direct the U.S. Department of Agriculture, working with the Department of Health and Human Services, to create a pilot grant program that funds community organizations working to improve people's health through food. Eligible organizations — such as food banks and community health clinics working together — could use the money for things like delivering medically appropriate meals, writing "produce prescriptions," teaching cooking skills, and providing transportation to program participants. The program targets people who have, are at risk of, or are recovering from health conditions linked to diet, including diabetes, heart disease, obesity, cancer, and mental illness, among others. Up to $20 million is authorized for the program from 2027 through 2031, and the Agriculture Department would be required to submit progress reports to Congress assessing how well the program works and whether it improves participants' health.
ai_headline: USDA food-as-medicine pilot grants proposed for 2027
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Agriculture, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Economy of the Future Commission Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill creates a temporary, bipartisan advisory commission made up of 10 members appointed by congressional leaders — some of whom are current members of Congress and others who are outside experts in areas like artificial intelligence, education, workforce training, or taxation — along with four non-voting deputy secretaries from the Departments of Education, Labor, Commerce, and Treasury. The commission's job is to study how the spread of artificial intelligence is changing the economy — including effects on jobs, education, job retraining programs, taxes, manufacturing, transportation, and government services — and then produce recommendations for new laws to address those changes. Within about seven months the commission must release a public interim report that includes estimates of how AI may affect employment and federal tax revenue five and ten years out, and within about thirteen months it must deliver a final report with specific legislative recommendations to key congressional committees and four Cabinet secretaries, who then have 60 days to respond with their own assessments. The commission is funded with $5.25 million, and it shuts down 120 days after delivering its final report. Ordinary Americans are affected indirectly, since the commission's work could shape future laws on job retraining programs, unemployment insurance, education, and how AI-driven industries are taxed.
ai_headline: Bill would create AI economic impact advisory panel
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

title: Healthy Competition for Better Care Act
ai_summary: This bill would make it illegal for health insurance companies and employer health plans to sign contracts with hospitals, doctors' networks, or other health care providers that contain certain clauses limiting competition. Specifically, it bans contract terms that prevent an insurer from steering patients toward cheaper or higher-quality providers, force an insurer to also contract with a hospital's or provider's affiliated facilities at set prices, or stop other insurers from negotiating lower prices than what one insurer already agreed to pay. There are exceptions for certain coordinated-care arrangements — such as HMOs that work through exclusive physician groups, accountable care organizations, and centers of excellence — where such restrictions may still be allowed because those models are designed around integrated, quality-focused care. States can also temporarily shield certain specific contracts that were signed around 2019–2020 from the new rules for up to ten years, but only if the state determines those contracts are unlikely to significantly harm competition. The rules would apply to new, renewed, or amended contracts starting 18 months after the law is enacted, and the relevant federal agencies must issue regulations within one year of enactment.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets anticompetitive health insurance contracts
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

title: Stop Militarizing Our Streets Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill places new restrictions on the Department of Defense (DoD) regarding the sale and purchase of certain firearms and ammunition in the civilian marketplace. Specifically, it would prohibit the DoD and government-owned weapons plants from selling "military-grade assault weapons" (defined as semi-automatic, gas- or recoil-operated firearms capable of accepting magazines holding more than 10 rounds) or ammunition of .22 caliber or larger into the commercial market, and it would bar the DoD from buying weapons or ammunition from any manufacturer or dealer that sells those prohibited items commercially. For other firearms and ammunition that are not in the prohibited category, the bill would require dealers to meet a detailed set of conditions — including holding proper licenses, completing employee training, maintaining electronic inventory records, capping how much ammunition can be sold to one person in 30 days, waiting for background check clearance before any sale, and following specific security and anti-theft practices — before the DoD can do business with them. The bill also creates a new federal licensing system for ammunition dealers (similar to the existing system for gun dealers), allows licensed ammunition dealers to use the national background check system, and requires annual public reports to Congress on commercial firearms and ammunition sales by both government plants and the DoD. The bill primarily affects the DoD, government-owned munitions plants, and private firearms and ammunition manufacturers and dealers who want to sell to or buy from the federal government.
ai_headline: DoD gun sales face new civilian market limits
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Armed Services, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: WELLS Act
ai_summary: This bill would require hospitals, critical access hospitals, and rural emergency hospitals that participate in Medicare to create a formal discharge plan before sending home any pregnant patient who is showing signs of labor but has not yet delivered. The discharge plan must include a medical reason for the discharge, information about how far the patient lives from the hospital, confirmation that reliable transportation is available, identification of a backup hospital where the patient could receive labor and delivery care if needed, and confirmation that all of this information was explained to the patient in their own language. The bill also expands an existing federal grant program that trains rural healthcare workers in maternal care, adding a requirement that the training include racial bias education and that grant recipients meet specific performance targets to keep receiving funding. Additionally, the bill directs the federal government to launch a research initiative comparing different ways of training healthcare professionals in maternal care, and to create a publicly available online dashboard tracking national data on maternal health outcomes, hospital discharges of pregnant patients before delivery, and federal spending on maternal health research.
ai_headline: Hospitals would need discharge plans for laboring patients
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Trade Adjustment Assistance Modernization Act
ai_summary: This bill revives and updates a set of federal programs that help people, businesses, farmers, and communities hurt by foreign trade competition. Workers who lose jobs because of trade — including temporary staffers, remote workers, and government employees — could receive extended income payments, free job training, and new allowances to cover job-search costs, relocation expenses, and child or dependent care, with dollar limits on several of those benefits raised and tied to inflation going forward. Businesses facing trade-related hardship could apply for up to $300,000 in federal assistance to restructure their operations, and entire communities economically damaged by trade could apply for grants of up to $25 million to fund infrastructure, job development, and recovery plans. Farmers affected by trade shifts, community colleges serving displaced workers, and workers in U.S. territories such as Guam and the Virgin Islands are also brought into coverage under the updated programs. Overall, the bill affects workers across a wide range of industries and situations, small and mid-sized businesses, local governments, agricultural producers, and the federal and state agencies that administer these assistance programs.
ai_headline: Bill expands Trade Adjustment Assistance benefits
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

title: DISCLOSE Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would require corporations, labor unions, Super PACs, and other politically active organizations to publicly disclose who is funding their election-related spending, including the names and addresses of major donors who give $10,000 or more. It also strengthens the existing ban on foreign money in U.S. elections by closing loopholes — for example, by covering foreign spending on state ballot measures, online political ads, and efforts to influence federal court nominations — and creates a new federal crime of setting up a shell company specifically to hide foreign political contributions. Political ads paid for by these organizations would have to include an on-screen or spoken statement identifying the top funders behind the communication, similar to the "I approve this message" disclosures already required of candidates. The bill affects a wide range of groups — including nonprofit advocacy organizations, trade associations, and politically active clubs — as well as any foreign governments or their agents who might try to spend money influencing American elections or judicial appointments.
ai_headline: Bill would require donors behind political ads to register
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on House Administration, and in addition to the Committees on Ways and Means, and the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Secure Tracks Act
ai_summary: This bill sets new minimum rules for how often railroad tracks must be physically inspected by trained inspectors and scanned by automated measurement equipment. For busier or faster main line tracks (Class 3 and above), a qualified human inspector must walk or ride the track at least twice a week, with at least one day between visits, and must immediately begin fixing any safety problem found — and only that inspector can authorize trains to move on a track that has been taken out of service for repairs. The bill also requires the Department of Transportation to update its regulations within one year to mandate that automated Track Geometry Measurement System (TGMS) machines — which electronically measure the shape and alignment of the rails — run over tracks on a schedule that varies based on track class and how much freight they carry, ranging from a few times a year on lower-traffic lines to twice every 30 days on the highest-speed tracks. Additionally, the bill prevents the federal government from granting waivers that would allow an alternative inspection method if that method cannot detect all conditions already recognized as unsafe under existing rules. These requirements affect freight and passenger railroads operating across the country, the railroad workers who perform inspections, and ultimately the communities and passengers who depend on safe rail travel.
ai_headline: HR 7784 tightens railroad track inspection schedules
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

title: To authorize the Secretary of Housing and Urban Development to prioritize the award of certain housing grants to applicants located in, or serving, low-income communities.
ai_summary: This bill gives the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) the option to favor certain grant applicants whose housing projects are located in, or would significantly benefit, officially designated low-income areas known as "opportunity zones." Specifically, it applies to competitive housing grants — meaning grants where applicants compete against each other — that fund building, modifying, fixing up, or preserving housing. Under this bill, HUD could give extra consideration (described as "additional weight") to those applicants when deciding who receives the money, though it is not required to do so. The people most directly affected would be organizations and developers applying for HUD housing grants, as well as residents of low-income opportunity zone communities who might see more housing investment come to their neighborhoods as a result.
ai_headline: HUD may favor opportunity zone housing grant bids
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

title: PrEP Access and Coverage Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would require nearly all health insurance programs in the United States — including private insurance, Medicare, Medicaid, CHIP, the Federal Employees Health Benefits Program, VA health care, and TRICARE for military members — to cover FDA-approved HIV prevention medications (known as PrEP and PEP) along with the related lab tests, doctor visits, and follow-up care, all at no out-of-pocket cost to the patient. Insurance plans generally could not require prior approval before covering these services, though a plan may require prior approval for a specific brand-name drug as long as it covers at least one equivalent alternative without prior approval. The bill also makes it illegal for life, disability, or long-term care insurers to deny coverage, raise premiums, or otherwise penalize someone simply because they take HIV prevention medication. Additionally, the bill directs the government to fund public education campaigns aimed at increasing awareness of PrEP and PEP — especially in communities most affected by HIV — and to award grants to states, tribes, and nonprofit organizations to help uninsured and underinsured people access these medications. Anyone harmed by a violation of the bill's requirements could sue in federal court, and if they win, the defendant would be required to pay their legal costs.
ai_headline: Bill would mandate no-cost PrEP coverage across insurers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

title: Housing for America’s Middle Class Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill directs the Government Accountability Office (GAO) — a federal watchdog agency — to conduct a study and deliver a report to Congress within one year on housing challenges faced by middle-income households, defined as families earning between 80% and 120% of the median family income in their area. The report must identify the specific barriers these households face in finding affordable housing, pinpoint the parts of the country where housing is most out of reach for them, and list existing federal housing programs (such as tax credits, grants, and loans) that they are currently ineligible for because their incomes are considered too high. The GAO must also recommend a standard federal definition for the term "workforce housing" and analyze how existing or new federal programs could be adjusted to serve this middle-income group, assuming funding were made available. This bill does not create any new housing programs or provide any money — it only requires the study and recommendations to be written and sent to Congress.
ai_headline: GAO ordered to study middle-income housing gaps
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

title: HOME Expansion Act
ai_summary: This bill makes three main changes to a federal program that helps communities fund affordable housing. First, it allows smaller towns and rural areas that don't already receive a separate type of federal community development funding to use HOME program money to fix or install nearby infrastructure — like water lines, sewer systems, sidewalks, and roads — as long as that infrastructure is directly connected to affordable housing in the area. Second, it raises the maximum price at which a home can be sold and still count as "affordable" under the program, changing the limit from 95% to 110% of the area's median purchase price, which means slightly more expensive homes can qualify. Third, it creates two new exceptions to the income rules for homeowners: military members who are deployed far from home or receive permanent reassignment orders can temporarily have their income requirements waived, and family members who inherit a HOME-assisted home can keep its affordable housing status as long as they live there as their primary residence and take on the original owner's obligations under the program.
ai_headline: Senate bill expands HOME affordable housing program rules
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

title: End Veteran Homelessness Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill makes several changes to the HUD-VASH program, a federal program that provides rental vouchers and support services to help homeless veterans find and keep housing. It clarifies that case managers — staff who connect veterans to housing, health care, and other benefits — should be assigned only to veterans who actually need that help, and requires that the most vulnerable veterans (such as those with serious mental illness, substance abuse disorders, or physical disabilities) be helped first. The bill also adds new protections so that veterans who decline case management cannot lose their rental vouchers or be evicted solely for that reason, and it allows veterans who don't need case management at all to still use a voucher. Finally, it requires the Department of Veterans Affairs and the Department of Housing and Urban Development to submit annual reports to Congress tracking how the program is working — including how many vouchers are being used, who is being served, and what barriers exist — and directs the Government Accountability Office to conduct an independent study of the program within one year.
ai_headline: Bill updates rules for HUD-VASH homeless veteran vouchers
last_action_text: Forwarded by Subcommittee to Full Committee by Voice Vote.

title: Tribal Conservation Priorities Inclusion Act
ai_summary: This bill changes existing federal farm conservation law to formally include Indian Tribes alongside state and local governments when it comes to identifying and addressing priority conservation needs on agricultural land. Right now, several federal programs — which pay farmers and landowners to adopt environmentally friendly practices like improving water quality, reducing erosion, or protecting wildlife habitat — allow state and local governments to help set the conservation priorities for their areas, but do not explicitly mention tribal governments or tribal lands. This bill adds tribes and tribal lands to those programs so that tribal governments can have the same standing as states and localities in shaping conservation goals for land within their territories. The people most directly affected are tribal governments and people who farm or manage land on tribal lands, who would gain clearer access to these conservation programs and a recognized voice in setting their priorities.
ai_headline: Tribal governments gain standing in USDA farm conservation programs
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

title: Superfund Area Facts and Exposure Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the Government Accountability Office (GAO) — the federal government's independent research and auditing agency — to count how many homes are located within one mile of federally designated Superfund sites, which are places the government has identified as seriously contaminated with hazardous materials. The study must separately count how many of those nearby homes are public housing units. Within six months of the bill becoming law, the GAO must deliver its findings to Congress in a written report. The bill affects anyone who lives near a Superfund site, particularly residents of public housing, and it gives lawmakers factual data about how many people may be living close to these contaminated areas.
ai_headline: GAO ordered to map homes near Superfund sites
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

title: To designate the facility of the United States Postal Service located at 4431 Main Street in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, as the "Dr. Constance 'Connie' E. Clayton Post Office".
ai_summary: This bill gives an official honorary name to a specific post office building. If passed, the U.S. Postal Service facility at 4431 Main Street in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, would be officially renamed the "Dr. Constance 'Connie' E. Clayton Post Office." Any official government documents, maps, or records that refer to that building would use the new name going forward. The bill does not change how the post office operates, affect its services, or involve any spending — it is purely a naming designation. It mainly affects how that single Philadelphia post office is identified in official government records.
ai_headline: Philadelphia post office renamed for Dr. Connie Clayton
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

title: Jaime’s Law
ai_summary: This bill would require background checks for ammunition purchases, extending the same system already used for firearm purchases. Under the bill, when one private individual wants to sell or give ammunition to another private individual, the transaction must first go through a federally licensed gun dealer, importer, or manufacturer, who runs a background check to make sure the buyer is legally allowed to have ammunition. If the buyer is prohibited under federal or state law — for example, due to a felony conviction — the sale cannot proceed. The bill includes exceptions for certain situations, such as temporary transfers between family members, loans at shooting ranges, transfers needed to prevent imminent harm, and use during hunting or fishing when the transferor has no reason to believe the recipient will misuse the ammunition. The bill affects anyone who buys or sells ammunition privately, as well as licensed dealers who would take on the role of processing these checks, and it explicitly states it does not create a national ammunition registry.
ai_headline: Bill would extend background checks to ammunition sales
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: United States-Cuba Trade Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would end the decades-long U.S. trade embargo against Cuba by repealing or removing a wide range of laws and regulations that have restricted trade, travel, and financial dealings between the two countries. If enacted, Americans would be free to travel to Cuba and spend money there without special government permission, U.S. companies could sell goods and services to Cuba and install telecommunications equipment on the island, and money transfers (remittances) to Cuba would no longer be capped at a limited amount. Cuban goods would be treated the same as goods from most other countries when it comes to U.S. import taxes, and U.S. phone and internet carriers could operate between the two countries. The bill also directs the President to pursue negotiations with Cuba over property that was seized from U.S. citizens and over human rights protections, and requires a report to Congress on U.S.-Cuba trade within a year and a half of passage.
ai_headline: Bill would repeal U.S. trade embargo against Cuba
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committees on Ways and Means, Energy and Commerce, the Judiciary, Agriculture, and Financial Services, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: SPARK Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the Small Business Administration (SBA) to create two new programs that support small business incubators and accelerators — organizations that help early-stage businesses grow — specifically in communities that have historically had less access to business resources. The first program would have the SBA sign funding agreements with qualifying organizations (such as nonprofits, community lenders, minority banks, and community colleges) to run 5-year projects offering free mentorship, counseling, and business development services to startups and small businesses, with each funded organization receiving at least $500,000 per year. The second program would give those same types of organizations up to $1 million per year (or up to $500,000 for organizations not in the first program) to pass along as small grants (capped at $20,000 each) or low-interest loans directly to qualifying small businesses — at no fee to the business owner. Both programs are aimed at helping entrepreneurs who are women, racial minorities, veterans, people with disabilities, formerly incarcerated individuals, rural residents, or located in economically distressed areas, and the SBA would be required to track outcomes, report to Congress annually, and set up rules to prevent fraud.
ai_headline: SBA bill targets incubator funding for underserved communities
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Small Business and Entrepreneurship.

title: Stop Price Gouging in Grocery Stores Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would place new rules on grocery stores and other retail food sellers, making it illegal for them to charge prices that are far above what those items normally cost — a practice it calls "price gouging" — and also making it illegal for stores to use customers' personal information (such as data gathered by cameras or facial recognition technology) to charge different people different prices for the same item. Stores larger than 10,000 square feet would be required to use traditional physical price tags (like paper stickers or shelf labels) instead of electronic digital price displays. If a store uses facial recognition cameras, it must post a clear sign at the entrance telling customers about it, and any use of a customer's biometric data (like a fingerprint or face scan) requires the customer's written consent and strict limits on how that data can be shared. People who are harmed by these practices can sue the store directly in federal court, state attorneys general can bring cases on behalf of residents, and the Federal Trade Commission would be responsible for enforcing the law and writing the specific rules that define when a price crosses the line into illegal territory.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets grocery price gouging and biometric data use
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

title: After Hours Child Care Act
ai_summary: This bill would create a new federal pilot program, run by the Department of Health and Human Services, to help working parents who have jobs outside normal 9-to-5 weekday hours — such as evening, night, or weekend shifts — find and afford child care. Through the program, grants of between $25,000 and $500,000 would be awarded competitively over five-year periods to child care providers, businesses, or partnerships of those organizations to start new child care programs, expand existing ones, improve facilities, hire staff, or help providers meet licensing and safety requirements. To receive a grant, organizations must cover 25 percent of the costs themselves, with the federal government paying the remaining 75 percent. The program is designed to benefit parents who struggle to find child care during nonstandard hours, with the goal of helping them stay employed, earn promotions, and build savings. At least every two years, the department would be required to report to Congress on how many children were served and how the grants affected child care availability.
ai_headline: HHS pilot would fund odd-hours child care grants
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

title: To amend the Food, Agriculture, Conservation, and Trade Act of 1990 to ensure adequate staffing and resources for the Institute of Tropical Forestry and the Institute of Pacific Islands Forestry.
ai_summary: This bill would require the federal government to maintain minimum staffing levels at two U.S. Forest Service research facilities — the Institute of Tropical Forestry in Puerto Rico and the Institute of Pacific Islands Forestry in Hawaii. Specifically, the Secretary of Agriculture would be required by law to keep at least 50 employees at the Puerto Rico institute and at least 30 employees at the Pacific Islands institute, along with enough resources for both locations to meaningfully carry out their scientific research, demonstration projects, and sharing of forestry knowledge. The bill primarily affects federal agency staffing decisions and the communities, scientists, and land managers in Puerto Rico and the Pacific Islands who rely on the research and expertise these institutes provide. Currently, the law contains no such minimum staffing requirements, so this change would set a legal floor that the government must meet.
ai_headline: Bill sets minimum staffing floors for two Forest Service labs
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

title: Airborne Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill creates a new federal tax credit for businesses, public entities, and nonprofit organizations that improve the air quality inside their buildings. Owners of commercial properties, government-owned buildings, and 501(c)(3) nonprofits could claim a credit of $1 per square foot for getting a professional indoor air quality assessment, $5 per square foot for installing or repairing an air cleaning system, and $50 per square foot for upgrading a heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) system — with those per-square-foot amounts jumping to $25 and $250 respectively if the installation work meets federal prevailing wage and apprenticeship requirements. To qualify, any air cleaning or HVAC upgrade must be professionally certified as meeting specific industry ventilation standards and must have followed an indoor air quality assessment first. The credit for upgrades is capped at 50 percent of what the taxpayer actually spent on those improvements, and the credit for assessments is capped at the actual cost of the assessment. The bill also directs the Department of Energy to set up a voluntary certification program so property owners can formally verify their buildings meet the required air quality standards.
ai_headline: New tax credits target indoor air quality upgrades in commercial buildings
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

title: SELF DRIVE Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill sets new federal safety rules for cars and trucks that can drive themselves — vehicles equipped with what the bill calls "automated driving systems" (self-driving technology at higher levels of automation). Companies that make or sell these self-driving vehicles in the United States would be required to prepare a detailed "safety case" — a documented explanation of how their system works, what conditions it's designed to handle, how it responds to emergencies and vulnerable people like pedestrians and cyclists, and how it protects against hacking — and must provide that document to federal transportation officials upon request. The bill also creates a national database where manufacturers must report serious crashes involving self-driving systems within a set time window, and requires them to regularly report how many miles their vehicles have traveled with the self-driving system active. States and cities would generally be blocked from creating their own conflicting rules about the design or sale of these vehicles, though they would keep their existing authority over traffic laws, vehicle registration, insurance, and similar matters. The bill also directs federal officials to review existing rules about the security of connected vehicles and report back to Congress.
ai_headline: Federal safety rules set for self-driving vehicles
last_action_text: Forwarded by Subcommittee to Full Committee by the Yeas and Nays: 12 - 11.

title: Disaster Zone Energy Affordability and Investment Act
ai_summary: This bill lets certain businesses that already have unused energy tax credits — credits they earned in prior years but couldn't fully use — sell or transfer those credits to other taxpayers, as long as the business is spending money to operate in an area hit by a federally declared disaster or a governor-declared state disaster that occurred after December 31, 2023. Under current tax law, businesses can "carry forward" unused energy-related tax credits to future tax returns, but those carryforwards generally cannot be sold or transferred to someone else; this bill creates an exception for disaster-affected businesses. The amount that can be transferred is capped at whatever the business actually spends on its operations in the disaster area, and those qualifying expenses must be made within two years after the disaster declaration. This primarily affects businesses — not individual households — that hold unused general business energy credits and are rebuilding or continuing operations in a federally or state-recognized disaster zone.
ai_headline: Disaster-hit businesses could sell unused energy tax credits
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

title: Community Health Center Drug Pricing Protection Act
ai_summary: This bill requires that when community health clinics (known as federally qualified health centers) buy outpatient prescription drugs, they must be charged the discounted "340B" price **at the moment of purchase** — they cannot be made to pay full price upfront and wait for a refund or rebate later. The 340B program is an existing federal program that entitles certain safety-net health providers to buy drugs at significantly reduced prices, but some drug manufacturers have required these clinics to pay higher prices at the register and then seek reimbursement afterward. This bill closes that gap by prohibiting the federal government from signing agreements with drug manufacturers that allow any such "pay full price now, get money back later" arrangement. The people most directly affected are the low-income and underserved patients who rely on federally qualified health centers, since these clinics depend on immediate drug cost savings to stretch their budgets and serve their communities. The rule applies to drug purchases made from the day the law is enacted and also affects agreements with manufacturers that are already in place.
ai_headline: Bill requires upfront 340B discounts at health clinics
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

title: Purchased and Referred Care Improvement Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill strengthens protections for Native American patients who receive health care from outside doctors or hospitals that the Indian Health Service (IHS) has approved and referred them to. Under current law, patients are already not supposed to be billed personally for this care, but this bill makes that protection apply no matter what paperwork a patient may have signed, and it also extends that protection to debt collectors — not just the original provider. The bill also requires the IHS to notify both the patient and the outside provider within 5 business days that the patient owes nothing, and it creates a process for patients who paid out of pocket to be reimbursed by the IHS within 30 days of submitting their paperwork, either online or in person. Finally, the bill replaces the older term "contract health services" with "purchased/referred care" throughout federal health law, and it directs the government to update its manuals, contracts, and official documents to match the new rules within set timeframes.
ai_headline: Senate bill shields Native Americans from IHS referral bills
last_action_text: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held.

title: Child Care for Every Community Act
ai_summary: This bill would create a new nationwide government program providing child care and early learning services to all young children who are not yet old enough to be required to attend school. Any eligible child would have a legal right to participate — not just those whose families meet certain income limits — and the federal government would cover at least 90% of program costs, with families who pay fees capped at no more than 7% of their income. Local governments, states, tribal nations, and certain nonprofit organizations could apply to run the program in their area, and they would be required to offer full-day, full-year care that includes health, nutrition, mental health, and family support services alongside education. The bill specifically prioritizes serving low-income children, children with disabilities, children learning English as a second language, homeless children, and children of migrant farmworkers, and it sets rules requiring fair pay for child care workers and meaningful involvement of parents in how programs are run.
ai_headline: Bill would guarantee universal preschool for all U.S. children
last_action_text: ASSUMING FIRST SPONSORSHIP - Ms. Ocasio-Cortez asked unanimous consent that she may hereafter be considered as the first sponsor of H.R. 5658, a bill originally introduced by Representative Sherrill, for the purpose of adding cosponsors and requesting reprintings pursuant to clause 7 of rule XII. Agreed to without objection.

title: Protecting Small Businesses from Predatory Website Lawsuits Act
ai_summary: This bill changes the process that people with disabilities must follow before they can sue a private business over an inaccessible website or mobile app. Under the new process, a person would first have to notify the business directly that its site or app is not accessible; if the business doesn't fix the problem within 180 days, the person could then file a complaint with the U.S. Department of Justice, which would have up to 360 days to investigate; only after that entire process is completed — which could take well over a year — could the person file a lawsuit. Importantly, if the Justice Department finds the business is in compliance, or simply fails to reach a decision within its 360-day window, that counts as a final ruling in the business's favor and the person cannot sue. This affects people with disabilities who encounter inaccessible commercial websites or apps, and the private businesses that own or operate those digital properties.
ai_headline: Bill adds DOJ review step before disability web access suits
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues

title: ROUTE Act
ai_summary: This bill would allow truck drivers who are 18, 19, or 20 years old to drive commercial vehicles (like large semi-trucks) across state lines under specific conditions, even though federal law currently limits interstate commercial driving to those 21 and older. To qualify, a young driver must already hold a valid commercial driver's license for in-state driving in their home state. The interstate driving allowed under this bill is limited: the driver must stay within 150 miles (as the crow flies) of their home base, must return to that home base and be released from work within 14 consecutive hours, and must take at least 10 hours off before their next work period. This would primarily affect younger adults seeking careers in trucking and the employers — such as freight and delivery companies — who might hire them for regional routes that cross state lines.
ai_headline: Truckers aged 18–20 could haul freight across state lines
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

title: To designate the facility of the United States Postal Service located at 3135 1st Avenue North in St. Petersburg, Florida, as the "Poul Hornsleth Post Office".
ai_summary: This bill gives an official honorary name to a specific U.S. Post Office building. If passed, the post office located at 3135 1st Avenue North in St. Petersburg, Florida, would be officially renamed the "Poul Hornsleth Post Office." Any government documents, maps, or records that refer to that building would use the new name going forward. The bill directly affects only this one postal facility and has no impact on postal services, employees, or the general public beyond the name change.
ai_headline: Bill would rename St. Petersburg post office for Poul Hornsleth
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

title: Providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Department of Veterans Affairs relating to "Reproductive Health Services".
ai_summary: This resolution would cancel a rule issued by the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) on December 31, 2025, that dealt with reproductive health services. If passed by both the House and Senate and signed into law, the VA rule would be wiped off the books and could no longer be enforced. The resolution does not spell out what the VA rule itself required or allowed — it simply acts to nullify it entirely. Veterans who might have been affected by whatever reproductive health services that VA rule addressed would be the people most directly impacted by this cancellation.
ai_headline: House moves to repeal VA reproductive health rule
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Veterans' Affairs.

title: Gerald’s Law Act
ai_summary: This bill would make veterans eligible for a VA burial allowance if they die at home while receiving VA hospice care, as long as they had previously been receiving care in a VA hospital or VA-connected nursing home. Currently, the burial allowance is available to veterans who die inside those VA facilities, but not to those who transfer to at-home hospice care and pass away there. The change would close that gap so that dying at home — rather than in a facility — does not disqualify a veteran's family from receiving burial assistance. The bill would apply retroactively, as though it had been part of a veterans' health care law passed in 2020.
ai_headline: VA burial benefits would extend to home hospice veterans
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Veterans' Affairs.

title: Public Safety Communications Act
ai_summary: This bill creates a new office inside the National Telecommunications and Information Administration (NTIA), a federal agency that handles communications policy, specifically to focus on public safety communications. The new office would be led by a career government official called the Associate Administrator for Public Safety Communications, who would oversee federal grant programs for Next Generation 9-1-1 (the modernized version of the 911 emergency call system), advise on public safety communications policy, and manage the First Responder Network Authority, which is the organization that runs the nationwide broadband network used by police, firefighters, and other emergency responders. The office would also oversee government research and technology testing aimed at improving how first responders communicate during emergencies. This would primarily affect federal agencies, emergency responders, and local governments that rely on or apply for federal funding related to public safety communications systems.
ai_headline: NTIA would get a new public safety communications office
last_action_text: Forwarded by Subcommittee to Full Committee by Voice Vote.

title: Next Generation 9–1–1 Act
ai_summary: This bill directs a federal official (the Assistant Secretary of the National Telecommunications and Information Administration) to run a grant program that helps states, U.S. territories, and Native American tribes upgrade their 911 systems to "Next Generation 9-1-1" — a modern, internet-based system that can handle not just phone calls but also texts, photos, videos, and other data sent to emergency call centers. Grant money can be used for building and maintaining the new systems, training staff, telling the public how to use the new capabilities, and adding cybersecurity protections, with spending caps on training and administrative costs. To receive a grant, states and tribes must meet several requirements, including designating a single coordinator, submitting an upgrade plan, and certifying that local 911 fees are not being spent on unrelated things — and any entity that provides false information loses eligibility and must return the money. The bill also creates a national cybersecurity center focused specifically on protecting these 911 systems, and an advisory board of 16 public safety professionals (from law enforcement, fire and rescue, emergency medical services, and 911 operations) to advise on how the grants and rules should work. Funding is authorized for fiscal years 2026 through 2030, with no more than 4 percent of appropriated funds going to administrative costs.
ai_headline: Federal grants would fund Next Generation 9-1-1 upgrades
last_action_text: Forwarded by Subcommittee to Full Committee by Voice Vote.

title: Dietary Supplement Listing Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would require companies that make, package, or distribute dietary supplements sold in the United States to register each of their products with the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and provide detailed information about them, including the product's name, ingredients, serving sizes, directions, allergen warnings, and a copy of its label. Companies with products already on the market would have 18 months to comply, while new products would need to be registered before they go on sale; companies must also notify the FDA within a year if they stop selling a product and update their registration within 30 days if they change a label. The FDA would be required to create a publicly searchable online database where anyone could look up this information, though certain details — like contact information for company owners and the exact amounts of individual ingredients inside proprietary blends — would be kept confidential. A dietary supplement that hasn't been properly registered would be considered "misbranded," meaning it could be treated as an illegal product. This bill primarily affects dietary supplement manufacturers, distributors, and packagers, but also benefits consumers, healthcare providers, and researchers who want easier access to accurate information about what is in the supplements they use or study.
ai_headline: FDA would track and publish dietary supplement data
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions. (text: CR S259-260)

title: SHIFT Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how the federal government and regional fishery councils manage fish populations that are moving into new ocean areas due to changing environmental conditions. When fish stocks shift significantly into a new region — whether measured by where the fish are, where fishermen are catching them, or where fishing revenues are going — the government must formally recognize that shift and update who is responsible for managing that fishery, using the best available science to account for ecological changes like habitat and food web effects. The bill also tightens the rules for introducing new types of fishing or fishing gear, requiring a formal analysis showing that any new fishery won't seriously harm fish habitat, existing fisheries, or coastal fishing communities before it can be officially authorized. Every five years, regional councils must review their authorized fisheries and gear and propose any needed updates. The people most directly affected are commercial and recreational fishermen, fishing communities along the U.S. coasts, and the regional councils and federal agencies that manage marine fisheries.
ai_headline: Senate bill tracks fish as stocks shift to new waters
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

title: Find It Early Act
ai_summary: This bill would require health insurance plans to cover additional breast cancer screenings — such as 3D mammograms, ultrasounds, MRI scans, and other imaging — at no cost to the patient, meaning no copays, deductibles, or other out-of-pocket charges. The no-cost coverage would apply to two groups: people identified as being at higher risk for breast cancer (including those with dense breast tissue, based on established medical criteria), and people whose doctor determines they need screening based on factors like age, race, ethnicity, or personal or family medical history. The requirement would apply broadly across nearly all types of health coverage, including employer-sponsored plans, individual and marketplace insurance, Medicare, Medicaid, TRICARE (military health coverage), and VA health care for veterans. The frequency of covered screenings would follow guidelines set by the National Comprehensive Cancer Network, a medical organization that publishes cancer care standards. The bill would take effect on January 1, 2026.
ai_headline: Insurers would cover extra breast cancer scans at no cost
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.

title: Fiscal State of the Nation Act
ai_summary: Every year, the Treasury Department already submits an audited financial report on the federal government's finances to Congress. This bill would require Congress to hold a public joint hearing — with members of both the House and Senate Budget Committees present — where the Comptroller General (the head of the Government Accountability Office, a nonpartisan watchdog agency) would walk lawmakers through the findings of that audit, including information about deficits, surpluses, long-term debt projections, and the government's overall financial health. The hearing must be held within 45 working days of the Treasury submitting its report, must be open to the public and to media coverage, and any member of Congress — not just Budget Committee members — would be allowed to participate. The presentation itself must follow nonpartisan, fact-based standards set by the Government Accountability Office. This bill primarily affects members of Congress and the Comptroller General, though ordinary citizens would benefit from the increased public visibility into the government's financial condition.
ai_headline: Bill requires public GAO briefing on federal finances
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Rules.

title: Community Passport Services Access Act
ai_summary: This bill would allow public libraries that are organized as nonprofits, charities, or similar non-governmental organizations to officially accept passport applications and keep the fee they charge customers for that service. Right now, that fee-retention authority is limited to certain government offices and the U.S. Postal Service; this bill adds qualifying public libraries to that list. Libraries that were already acting as passport acceptance facilities before this bill becomes law would be formally authorized to collect and keep that fee within 30 days of enactment, as long as they were following State Department rules at the time. The people most directly affected are library patrons who want a convenient local place to submit a passport application, and the libraries themselves, which would gain a new source of revenue from the execution fees they collect.
ai_headline: Nonprofit libraries could soon accept passport applications
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

title: Public Integrity in Financial Prediction Markets Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would make it illegal for a wide range of federal government workers and officials to bet on prediction markets — online platforms where people place wagers on whether specific events will happen — when those bets involve government policy, government actions, or political outcomes. The ban applies to elected members of Congress, their staff, political appointees, and employees of federal agencies. The prohibition specifically targets situations where these individuals either already possess inside government information that the public doesn't have access to, or where their job gives them a reasonable opportunity to obtain such information. For example, a federal employee who knows in advance about an upcoming policy decision before it is announced publicly could not use that knowledge to profit by betting on a prediction market tied to that policy. The core concern the bill addresses is that people with access to non-public government information could have an unfair advantage when betting on political or policy outcomes.
ai_headline: Federal workers would be barred from political prediction market bets
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, and in addition to the Committee on House Administration, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: EB–5 Regional Center Program Advisory Committee Authorization Act
ai_summary: This bill creates a new advisory committee inside U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) to provide guidance and recommendations on the EB-5 Regional Center Program — the federal program that allows foreign investors to obtain U.S. visas by investing money in American businesses or projects that create jobs. The committee would have up to 35 members drawn from regional centers (the organizations that pool and manage EB-5 investments), city mayors, county and state economic development officials, and federal agencies, and would be organized into subcommittees covering topics like application processing times, job creation, and investment rules. It would meet at least four times a year, submit annual reports to the Secretary of Homeland Security and the USCIS Director, and provide quarterly briefings to Congress — though it could not make recommendations about specific individual visa cases. The people most directly affected are foreign investors seeking EB-5 visas, the regional centers that sponsor investment projects, and the communities — particularly rural areas, high-unemployment areas, and cities of various sizes — where those projects are located.
ai_headline: USCIS would gain an EB-5 investor visa advisory panel
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: National Landslide Preparedness Act Reauthorization Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill extends and updates a federal program aimed at reducing the dangers of landslides across the United States through at least 2030, and increases the dedicated funding for that program from $25 million to $35 million per year, with at least $10 million of that specifically required to go toward buying, installing, and maintaining early warning systems in high-risk areas.
